proposals.title: RT-Trajectory-2: Scaling General-Purpose Robot Learning with Visual Trajectory Priors Across Diverse Real-World Tasks
proposals.motivation: The RT-Trajectory method demonstrated that overlaying 2D trajectory sketches on visual inputs can significantly improve the generalization of vision-language-action models for robotic manipulation. However, its evaluation was limited to a relatively narrow set of tabletop tasks. To validate its claim as a general-purpose method for boosting robotic policy learning, it must be tested on a broader spectrum of real-world challenges, including long-horizon tasks, dynamic environments, and tasks requiring greater spatial reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the RT-Trajectory method's core mechanism—providing a visual trajectory prior—will robustly improve policy learning and generalization across a significantly wider and more complex set of real-world tasks, including those with multi-stage sequences and less structured environments.
proposals.method: We will extend the RT-Trajectory framework to a new, diverse benchmark suite of 50+ real-world tasks on physical robots. This suite will include long-horizon activities (e.g., 'prepare a simple meal'), tasks in cluttered, dynamic kitchens, and mobile manipulation. We will use the core RT-Trajectory architecture, where a trajectory generator (based on a pre-trained vision-language model like RT-2) produces 2D trajectory sketches over RGB images, which are then fed alongside the original images into a policy network (e.g., a Transformer-based VLA model like π0.5). We will systematically compare the performance of policies trained with and without the trajectory overlay across this new benchmark.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous, large-scale evaluation of visual trajectory priors for generalist robot policies. It will establish the empirical boundaries of the RT-Trajectory method's effectiveness and produce a new, open-source benchmark for evaluating generalization in real-world robotic learning. The findings will guide future research on scalable, data-efficient robot training.
proposals.novelty: While RT-Trajectory introduced the concept, its scaling to a comprehensive, high-difficulty real-world task suite is unexplored. This proposal systematically tests the method's limits and generality, moving beyond initial proof-of-concept demonstrations to a principled study of its scaling properties in complex, embodied settings.

proposals.title: SparseSketch-VLA: Ultra-Efficient Vision-Language-Action Models for Low-Cost Robots via Sketch-Based Tokenization
proposals.motivation: The scaling of vision-language-action (VLA) models, as seen in π0.5, has enabled impressive generalization but requires high-fidelity vision and significant compute, limiting deployment to expensive research platforms. To enable widespread real-world application, we must adapt these methods for low-cost robots with simple sensors (e.g., monocular RGB, low-resolution) and limited onboard compute. This work addresses the critical gap between high-capacity VLAs and the hardware constraints of affordable robotics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by combining an ultra-sparse, sketch-based visual tokenizer with the efficient discrete action tokenization of FAST, we can create a VLA policy that maintains robust open-world generalization on low-cost robots while drastically reducing computational and perceptual requirements.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage tokenization pipeline. First, a lightweight encoder will transform raw images into sparse, structural sketches using a learned edge and primitive detector, inspired by human sketching, to create a compact, task-relevant visual token sequence. Second, we will integrate this with the FAST framework's action tokenizer, training the resulting VLA model (SparseSketch-VLA) on a diverse dataset of robot tasks. The model will be distilled from a larger, pre-trained VLA teacher (like π0.5) and evaluated on low-cost hardware (e.g., Hello Robot Stretch, modified toy robots) with simple cameras.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a novel sketch-based visual tokenizer for resource-constrained robotics, 2) a fully functional, efficient VLA model deployable on low-cost platforms, and 3) an empirical analysis of the trade-offs between perceptual sparsity, compute, and task performance in embodied AI, providing a blueprint for accessible robot intelligence.
proposals.novelty: While prior work scales VLAs up in capacity, we uniquely scale them down in perceptual and computational cost via learned sketch tokenization, a departure from standard dense vision encoders. This explicitly targets the hardware constraints of low-cost robots, a largely unexplored direction for modern VLAs.

proposals.title: Scaling Laws for Vision-Language-Action Policies: Data Mixture, Architecture, and Emergent Capabilities
proposals.motivation: Recent work like π0.5 demonstrates that scaling vision-language-action (VLA) models with diverse robot data enables impressive open-world generalization. However, the scaling laws governing these models—how performance scales with dataset size, diversity, and model parameters—remain poorly understood, especially compared to pure language or vision-language domains. Determining these relationships is critical for efficiently allocating resources to build more capable and generalist embodied agents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that scaling VLA transformer policies along axes of model size, dataset size, and dataset diversity (e.g., mixing real and synthetic data, multiple robot embodiments, and auxiliary internet video) will yield predictable, power-law improvements in multi-task performance and lead to the emergence of new compositional skills not present in the training data.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled scaling study, building on the FAST tokenizer and the π0.5 architecture. We will systematically vary model size (from 100M to 10B+ parameters), dataset scale (up to millions of robot trajectories), and data mixture ratios (real robot data, simulation rollouts, and passive human video with pseudo-labeled actions). Training will use a masked modeling objective on action tokens and a contrastive loss aligning video, language, and action sequences. We will evaluate scaling trends on a suite of benchmarks covering seen tasks, unseen task zero-shot generalization, and long-horizon reasoning.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first empirical scaling laws for embodied VLA models, providing a quantitative framework for future research. It will identify the most efficient scaling axis (data vs. model size) and the role of data diversity, culminating in a new state-of-the-art, openly released VLA policy that demonstrates emergent compositional task execution.
proposals.novelty: While scaling laws exist for LLMs and VLMs, this is the first systematic investigation focused on the action-generating VLA domain, where the optimal scaling strategy and the interplay between different data modalities are unknown and non-trivial.

proposals.title: Efficient Embodied Adaptation via Sparse, Hierarchical Action Token Finetuning
proposals.motivation: Large Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models like π0.5 demonstrate impressive open-world generalization, but adapting them to new robot embodiments or tasks remains costly, requiring extensive demonstration data and full-model finetuning. This bottleneck limits the scalable, real-world deployment of VLAs across diverse hardware platforms. Efficient adaptation is crucial for making generalist robot policies practically useful.
proposals.hypothesis: By exploiting the hierarchical and sparse structure of learned action token spaces in VLAs, we can develop parameter-efficient finetuning methods that adapt policies to new embodiments with orders of magnitude less data and compute than full-model finetuning.
proposals.method: We will extend the FAST action tokenization framework to enable sparse, hierarchical adaptation. First, we will analyze the learned action token space of a pre-trained VLA (e.g., π0.5) to identify a sparse set of 'embodiment-critical' token layers and attention heads. We will then develop a hierarchical Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) technique that applies adapters selectively to these sparse components and to the vision-language backbone, freezing all other parameters. Evaluation will benchmark data efficiency and performance against full finetuning on diverse robot platforms from the RT-X dataset.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a concrete, parameter-efficient finetuning algorithm for VLAs that drastically reduces adaptation cost. It will provide empirical scaling laws for embodiment adaptation data/compute requirements. The resulting method will be a key step towards practical, scalable deployment of generalist robot policies.
proposals.novelty: While parameter-efficient finetuning (e.g., LoRA) exists in NLP/vision, its application to VLAs for embodiment adaptation is unexplored. The key novelty is the co-design of sparsity and hierarchy derived from the structure of the action tokenizer itself, moving beyond naive application of existing adapters.

proposals.title: Diffusion Policies for Vision-Language-Action Models: Enabling Robust, Multi-Task Robot Control
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language-action (VLA) models for robotics, such as π0.5, often rely on deterministic or autoregressive action decoders. These methods can struggle with the inherent multimodality of action distributions in complex, contact-rich tasks, leading to suboptimal or unstable behaviors. Diffusion probabilistic models have demonstrated remarkable success in capturing complex, high-dimensional distributions in image and text generation, suggesting a powerful but underexplored alternative for robotic policy learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a diffusion-based action decoder into a VLA model architecture will produce more robust and higher-performing multi-task robotic policies by explicitly modeling the multimodality of action sequences and improving temporal coherence.
proposals.method: We will extend the VLA framework from π0.5, replacing its action tokenization and decoder with a diffusion-based policy. The model will ingest visual observations and language instructions, processed by a pre-trained vision-language encoder (e.g., CLIP). The action decoder will be a conditional diffusion model that iteratively denoises a sequence of continuous robot actions over a learned noise schedule. We will train the model on large-scale, multi-task robot datasets (e.g., Open X-Embodiment) using a simplified objective similar to DDPM, conditioning the denoising process on the encoded visual-language context and previous actions for temporal consistency.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, state-of-the-art class of VLA models with diffusion-based action generation, demonstrating improved success rates and robustness on diverse manipulation benchmarks. It will provide a systematic analysis of how diffusion sampling dynamics affect policy performance and exploration, contributing new insights to the fields of robot learning and generative models.
proposals.novelty: While diffusion models have been applied to low-level control and trajectory generation, their integration as the core action decoder within a scalable, instruction-conditioned VLA model for generalist robot policies is novel. This approach fundamentally shifts from discrete or deterministic action prediction to learning a smooth, generative action distribution conditioned on rich multimodal context.

proposals.title: RAPTOR: Retrieval-Augmented Policies for Task-Oriented Robot Generalization
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language-action (VLA) models, such as π0.5, are trained on fixed datasets, limiting their ability to adapt to new tasks or environments without costly retraining. Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) has shown success in NLP for grounding models in external knowledge, but its application to robotic action generation remains underexplored. This work addresses the critical need for robot policies that can efficiently leverage a large, growing corpus of demonstrations to improve generalization and sample efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: Augmenting a transformer-based VLA policy with a nearest-neighbor retrieval mechanism over a large, diverse dataset of robot demonstrations will enable more data-efficient adaptation to novel instructions and environments, outperforming fixed-parameter models on open-world generalization benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will extend the π0.5 VLA model architecture by integrating a retrieval module. For a given language instruction and image observation, we will use a pretrained vision-language encoder (e.g., CLIP) to embed the context and retrieve the k-most-similar (state, action) pairs from a large-scale dataset like Open X-Embodiment. These retrieved demonstrations will be tokenized (inspired by FAST's action tokenization) and prepended as context to the transformer policy's input sequence. The model will be trained to condition its action predictions on both the current input and the retrieved exemplars, using a contrastive loss to align retrieval and policy embeddings.
proposals.contribution: This research will yield a novel robotic policy architecture that dynamically grounds decision-making in relevant past experience, providing a scalable path to improve generalization without full model retraining. It will contribute a new benchmark for evaluating retrieval-augmented robot policies and insights into the role of episodic memory in embodied agents.
proposals.novelty: While RAG is established in NLP, its integration into end-to-end trainable VLA policies for continuous robot control is novel. This approach moves beyond using retrieval for high-level planning or code generation, directly retrieving and conditioning on low-level action demonstrations to influence closed-loop policy execution.

proposals.title: Inverse Dynamics from Generalist Policies: Scaling Inverse Model Learning with Synthetic Data
proposals.motivation: Generalist vision-language-action (VLA) policies, like π0.5, can perform a wide range of tasks but are monolithic and difficult to introspect or adapt. Inverse models, which predict actions from state transitions, are crucial for planning and model-based control but are notoriously data-inefficient to learn from real robot data. This creates a bottleneck for deploying flexible, modular reasoning systems in robotics.
proposals.hypothesis: A generalist VLA policy, trained on diverse real-world data, implicitly encodes a powerful prior over feasible actions and can be used to generate high-quality, scalable synthetic data for training sample-efficient inverse dynamics models.
proposals.method: We will leverage a pre-trained, frozen VLA policy (e.g., a π0.5 variant) as a synthetic data generator. By sampling language instructions and initial image observations from a broad distribution, we will roll out the policy in a learned world model or simulator to produce synthetic trajectories of (state, action, next_state) tuples. This dataset will then train a specialized, modular inverse dynamics model (e.g., a transformer-based network). We will evaluate the inverse model's accuracy and sample efficiency on held-out real robot data for locomotion and manipulation tasks, comparing it to models trained only on real data.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a novel paradigm for using generalist policies as data sources for more modular algorithms, effectively distilling their embodied knowledge. It will provide a scalable method for learning accurate inverse models, a key component for planning, and establish a new use case for VLAs beyond direct policy execution.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses policies for data augmentation or uses models to generate plans, the explicit use of a generalist VLA policy as a synthetic data engine for training a fundamentally different class of model (inverse dynamics) is unexplored. This inverts the typical data pipeline, using a policy to overcome the data scarcity of inverse problem learning.

proposals.title: Generalist from Specialists: Emergent Cross-Robot Capabilities from Limited Embodiment Data
proposals.motivation: Current large-scale vision-language-action (VLA) models, like RT-2 and π0.5, rely on massive, diverse datasets collected from many robot embodiments to achieve broad generalization. This data collection is a major bottleneck for scaling. This proposal investigates a critical, underexplored question: can a generalist policy with emergent cross-embodiment capabilities arise from training on data sourced from only a handful of robot types?
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a VLA model, when trained on a strategically selected, limited set of robot embodiments (e.g., a mobile manipulator and a dexterous hand), will develop latent representations and action abstractions that enable zero-shot generalization to unseen robot morphologies and tasks, challenging the necessity of extreme dataset diversity.
proposals.method: We will build upon the π0.5 VLA architecture and the FAST action tokenizer. We will curate two core training datasets: one from a mobile manipulator (e.g., Hello Robot Stretch) and one from a multi-fingered hand (e.g., Allegro). We will train a single model on this combined data, using a shared action vocabulary from FAST. Evaluation will test zero-shot performance on held-out robot types (e.g., a different manipulator arm, a quadruped) on both seen task families and novel instructions, measuring success rate and analyzing the learned latent space for shared structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic study of embodiment scaling laws in the low-diversity regime for VLAs. It will establish whether 'embodiment efficiency'—generalizing across morphologies from limited examples—is a viable path to capable generalist robots, potentially reducing data needs. The findings will offer concrete guidance on which robot types are most informative for pre-training.
proposals.novelty: While prior work scales up embodiment diversity, we deliberately scale it down to probe the limits of generalization. This inverts the dominant scaling paradigm to test if core physical and semantic concepts can be disentangled from specific kinematics through limited but complementary data sources.

proposals.title: π-Transfer: A Vision-Language-Action Policy for Zero-Shot Sim-to-Real Generalization Across Diverse Robot Morphologies
proposals.motivation: Current robot learning is bottlenecked by the need for extensive real-world data collection or per-robot policy adaptation, which is costly and impractical for scaling to many robots. While large-scale simulation training offers a path forward, the reality gap—the discrepancy between simulated and real dynamics—remains a major barrier to zero-shot transfer, especially across diverse robot embodiments. This work addresses the critical challenge of training a single, generalist policy in simulation that can deploy successfully on a wide variety of real robots without any fine-tuning.
proposals.hypothesis: A vision-language-action model, trained on a massive, procedurally generated distribution of simulated robot morphologies, dynamics parameters, and visual appearances, can learn representations and policies that are robust enough to transfer zero-shot to unseen real-world robots.
proposals.method: We will extend the π0.5 architecture, using a vision-language encoder and an action tokenizer (inspired by FAST), but train it on a new, expansive simulation benchmark. This benchmark will feature an order of magnitude more procedurally varied robot embodiments (legged, wheeled, manipulators) and randomized dynamics (friction, motor strengths) and visuals (lighting, textures). The core technical approach is to treat the robot's own embodiment as a latent variable inferred from its visual observations and proprioception, conditioning the policy on this inferred context alongside language instructions to achieve robust, morphology-aware control.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a single, generalist VLA policy capable of zero-shot execution of language-conditioned tasks on multiple, distinct real robots. It will also produce a large-scale, open-source simulation benchmark for studying embodiment generalization and provide empirical scaling laws for sim-to-real transfer performance as a function of morphological and dynamical diversity in training data.
proposals.novelty: While prior work in domain randomization addresses visual or dynamics gaps for a *single* robot morphology, this proposal uniquely focuses on scaling across *morphological* gaps, training one policy for many robots. It combines the representational power of VLAs with extreme embodiment randomization, a direction largely unexplored in pre-2026 sim-to-real research.

proposals.title: UniBot: Single-Robot Embodiment Generalization via Action Tokenization and Latent Morphology Alignment
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language-action (VLA) models require massive, diverse datasets from many robot platforms to achieve broad generalization, which is prohibitively expensive and impractical. This creates a fundamental bottleneck for scaling robot learning. A method that could learn generalizable policies from data collected on just a single robot would dramatically increase accessibility and enable rapid deployment of learned skills.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly separating the learning of abstract, platform-agnostic task semantics from low-level embodiment specifics, a VLA model trained on data from a single robot can generalize zero-shot to novel robot morphologies and action spaces.
proposals.method: We will extend the π0.5 VLA architecture with a novel two-stage action tokenization process. First, a high-level action tokenizer, inspired by FAST, will learn a discrete, abstract skill vocabulary from the single-robot data. Second, a lightweight, trainable 'Morphology Adapter' network will map these abstract tokens to the low-level action spaces of unseen robots, using only a static description (e.g., URDF, degrees-of-freedom) of the target platform. The model will be trained with a contrastive loss aligning the latent representations of language instructions, visual observations, and the abstract action tokens, decoupling them from the source robot's specific dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical framework for single-robot policy learning that generalizes across embodiments, significantly reducing data needs. It will provide new insights into the disentanglement of task and embodiment in VLA models and establish a benchmark for cross-platform generalization without multi-robot training data.
proposals.novelty: While prior work scales data across many robots, we explicitly avoid multi-robot data. Our novelty lies in forcing embodiment-agnostic abstraction via a disentangled action representation and a parameter-efficient adapter for zero-shot morphology transfer, a direction unexplored in pre-2026 VLA research.

proposals.title: End-to-End Embodied Learning: Scaling Vision-Language-Action Models Without Semantic Auxiliary Tasks
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language-action models (VLAs) for robotics, such as π0.5, often rely on auxiliary tasks like semantic subtask prediction to structure learning and improve sample efficiency. However, these hand-designed inductive biases may limit the model's ability to discover optimal, emergent behavioral abstractions directly from interaction data. As we scale embodiment data and model capacity, as explored in locomotion scaling laws, a critical question is whether such auxiliary supervision remains necessary or becomes a bottleneck for open-world generalization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a sufficiently large transformer-based VLA, trained on massive and diverse robot interaction data with an efficient action tokenization scheme like FAST, can learn complex, long-horizon manipulation and navigation policies in a purely end-to-end fashion—mapping high-dimensional observations to low-level actions—without any auxiliary semantic prediction losses, and will match or exceed the performance and generalization of auxiliary-supervised counterparts.
proposals.method: We will adapt the π0.5 architecture, removing its subtask prediction head and associated loss. The model will be trained end-to-end via behavior cloning or offline RL on large-scale datasets like Open X-Embodiment, using a next-action prediction objective. We will employ the FAST tokenizer to discretize continuous actions into a compact vocabulary, enabling efficient sequence modeling. Training will be conducted at scale, varying model size and dataset diversity to empirically test for emergent embodiment scaling laws in the absence of auxiliary supervision.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous, large-scale ablation demonstrating the necessity (or lack thereof) of semantic auxiliary tasks in modern VLAs. It will establish a cleaner, more minimalist baseline for end-to-end embodied learning and contribute new insights into the data and model scale required for policies to autonomously develop hierarchical structure from low-level action streams alone.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses auxiliary tasks as a crutch for learning, we systematically remove this crutch in the context of modern, large-scale VLAs and action tokenization—a non-obvious direction given the prevailing belief that such semantic structuring is essential for learning long-horizon tasks from pixels and language.

proposals.title: Scaling Semantic Priors: Training Vision-Language-Action Models on Massive Web and Synthetic Data
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language-action (VLA) models for robotics, such as π0.5, are trained primarily on limited, expensive robot demonstration data, which constrains their semantic and commonsense understanding of the world. This bottleneck limits open-world generalization to novel objects, environments, and task descriptions. Integrating the vast, diverse semantic knowledge from web-scale vision-language datasets and procedurally generated simulation data offers a promising path to break this data scarcity limitation, a key challenge in pre-2026 embodied AI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pre-training VLA model backbones on a massive, curated mixture of web image-text data and procedurally generated simulation frames with dense textual descriptions will create significantly stronger visual-semantic representations, leading to improved sample efficiency and generalization when fine-tuned on sparse real robot data.
proposals.method: We will extend the VLA architecture from π0.5/FAST, keeping the action tokenizer fixed. The vision-language backbone will be pre-trained on a hybrid dataset: (1) filtered web data (e.g., LAION) and (2) synthetic data from physics simulators (e.g., Isaac Sim) where scene graphs are rendered and automatically captioned with diverse language. We will employ a masked reconstruction objective for visual tokens and a contrastive loss (e.g., CLIP-style) for image-text alignment. This pre-trained model will then be efficiently adapted to robot control via residual adapter modules and fine-tuned on existing robot datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a scalable recipe for building more semantically grounded VLA policies, quantifying the gains in few-shot adaptation and generalization to novel instructions and environments. It will provide a systematic analysis of the trade-offs between web, simulation, and real robot data for embodied representation learning.
proposals.novelty: While web data is used for VLMs and simulation for RL, their combined, large-scale integration into an action-output VLA model for robotics is novel. The key innovation is the structured curation and blending of these non-robot data sources specifically to bootstrap semantic priors for robotic control, moving beyond using them only for independent perception modules.

proposals.title: Task-Specialized Action Tokenization for Efficient and Robust Robot Policy Learning
proposals.motivation: Recent vision-language-action models (VLAs) like π0.5 and FAST advocate for a universal action tokenizer, aiming for broad generalization. However, robot domains like dexterous manipulation and dynamic locomotion have fundamentally different action dynamics and temporal abstractions. A single tokenizer may be suboptimal, forcing a compromise between efficiency, precision, and sample complexity across diverse tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that learning task- or domain-specific action tokenizers, rather than a single universal one, will yield policies with higher final performance, faster training convergence, and improved robustness within their specialized operational envelope.
proposals.method: We will extend the FAST framework to learn a family of tokenizers. Using a multi-task robot dataset (e.g., spanning RT-1, Open X-Embodiment), we will cluster tasks by dynamic similarity (e.g., contact-rich vs. free-space motion). For each cluster, we will train a specialized VQ-VAE tokenizer, optimizing its codebook size and temporal receptive field. These tokenizers will condition a shared transformer backbone policy, and we will benchmark against a universal tokenizer baseline on in-domain and out-of-domain generalization tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic study of action tokenizer specialization in VLAs, establishing empirical scaling laws for tokenizer design per domain. It will deliver a practical framework for deploying efficient, high-performance robot policies by matching representation learning to physical task structure.
proposals.novelty: While current research pushes for universal tokenization, we challenge this one-size-fits-all premise. Our approach explicitly tailors the action representation to the physics of the task, a direction underexplored in pre-2026 VLA and embodiment scaling literature.

proposals.title: Unified Action-Observation Tokenization and Diffusion for Long-Horizon Robot World Models
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language-action (VLA) models, like π0.5, treat actions and observations as separate, discrete streams, limiting their ability to reason over the continuous, closed-loop dynamics of physical interaction. This makes long-horizon planning inefficient, as the model must generate actions step-by-step without an internal model of future observations. A unified representation that captures the joint distribution of actions and sensory outcomes is needed for efficient, multi-step reasoning in robotics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a joint, continuous tokenization of action-observation pairs, combined with a diffusion model trained to synthesize sequences in this space, will enable the generation of dynamically consistent, long-horizon sub-trajectories, thereby serving as an effective world model for planning.
proposals.method: We will first develop a continuous tokenizer, building on methods like FAST, to encode paired (image, action) vectors into a single token sequence. We will then train a diffusion transformer model on these unified sequences from offline robot datasets. The model will be conditioned on sparse, high-level rewards or goals provided by a frozen VLM (e.g., CLIP or a VLA policy). At inference, the model will perform iterative denoising to generate or infill sequences of tokens, which are decoded back into trajectories of actions and predicted image observations.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel joint tokenization scheme for continuous robot interaction data, 2) a diffusion-based world model capable of synthesizing plausible, goal-directed action-observation sequences, and 3) a new planning paradigm that reasons over complete interaction cycles, moving beyond single-step action prediction. This directly advances the scalability of embodied agents.
proposals.novelty: While prior work tokenizes actions and observations separately, our method fuses them into a single continuous representation and uses a diffusion process for direct sequence synthesis, enabling the model to 'imagine' coherent multi-step futures for planning, which is a departure from typical autoregressive next-token prediction in VLAs.

proposals.title: FAST-Hi: Hierarchical Action Tokenization for Scalable Vision-Language-Action Policies
proposals.motivation: Current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models like π0.5 and methods like FAST tokenize low-level motor actions, which struggles with long-horizon tasks requiring temporally extended reasoning. Scaling to complex, multi-stage robot manipulation and navigation demands a representation for abstract skills or sub-goals. A tokenization scheme for hierarchical action spaces is a critical missing component for enabling VLAs to plan and execute over extended time horizons.
proposals.hypothesis: Extending the FAST tokenization framework to represent temporally abstract skills, rather than only primitive actions, will enable more sample-efficient training and superior generalization of hierarchical VLA policies for long-horizon tasks.
proposals.method: We will adapt the FAST (Feature-based Action Sampling for Tokenization) framework to operate over a two-level action space. A high-level policy will output tokens representing discrete skills (e.g., 'pick block', 'navigate to door'), each defined by a latent goal embedding. A low-level, skill-conditioned policy, also trained with FAST, will generate the sequence of primitive action tokens to achieve that goal. We will train the hierarchical model end-to-end on datasets of long-horizon tasks, using a transformer architecture to process visual, language, and hierarchical action tokens.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel hierarchical action tokenization method for VLAs, 2) an empirical demonstration of improved efficiency and generalization on long-horizon robot benchmarks compared to flat tokenization, and 3) a scalable framework for bridging high-level language instructions with extended low-level action sequences.
proposals.novelty: While FAST efficiently tokenizes low-level actions, applying its core principles to create a unified tokenization scheme for a hierarchical policy—where high-level tokens correspond to learned, temporally extended skills—is a novel direction for VLA models.

proposals.title: Unified Discrete Latents for Vision-Language-Action: A Codebook Approach via FAST
proposals.motivation: Current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models often treat perception and action in separate, continuous spaces, limiting their ability to perform structured, compositional reasoning over long horizons. Discrete representations, as seen in generative models (e.g., VQ-VAE, DALL-E), offer benefits in generalization and sample efficiency but have not been deeply integrated for joint visual-action modeling in robotics. This work addresses the need for a unified, discrete latent space that can bridge high-level language instructions, visual observations, and low-level robot actions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training a shared, discrete codebook to jointly represent visual observations and executable action primitives within the FAST tokenization framework will enable more sample-efficient learning and better generalization for long-horizon, language-conditioned robot tasks compared to models operating in continuous or separate latent spaces.
proposals.method: We will extend the FAST (Efficient Action Tokenization) framework by integrating a vector-quantized variational autoencoder (VQ-VAE) to learn a single, discrete codebook from paired sequences of image observations and proprioceptive/action data. This codebook will serve as the unified vocabulary for a transformer-based VLA model. The model will be trained on diverse robotic datasets (e.g., Open X-Embodiment, RT-1) to perform language-conditioned action prediction, using cross-entropy loss over the discrete code indices. We will ablate the design against continuous and separate-discrete baselines.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a novel VLA architecture that leverages the representational efficiency and compositional structure of discrete latent variables for both perception and action. It will provide empirical evidence on the sample efficiency and generalization benefits of this approach for long-horizon tasks. The resulting model and codebook will be a step towards more data-efficient and interpretable robot learning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of vector quantization techniques from generative AI with the cross-modal tokenization of FAST to create a single, shared discrete space for vision and action, enabling a transformer to reason and plan with a unified, symbolic-like vocabulary.

proposals.title: FAST-Cache: Scaling Vision-Language-Action Tokenizers with Hierarchical Simulation Data Lakes
proposals.motivation: Scaling vision-language-action models (VLAs) like π0.5 and FAST requires massive, diverse datasets of robot sensory-motor trajectories, but generating this data via on-the-fly simulation is computationally prohibitive. This bottleneck limits our ability to discover embodiment scaling laws and achieve robust open-world generalization. A method to efficiently generate, cache, and sample from a universe of simulated robot experiences is needed to break this data ceiling.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical, distributed cache of pre-generated simulation trajectories, indexed by task complexity and environmental fidelity, will enable orders-of-magnitude scaling in tokenizer training data diversity and volume without a proportional increase in training-time compute, leading to more generalizable and data-efficient robot policies.
proposals.method: We will build a federated data lake by massively parallelizing simulation across diverse environments (e.g., Isaac Gym, Habitat) to generate sensory-motor trajectories at multiple resolution levels. We will adapt techniques from large-scale cosmological simulations to implement a hierarchical caching system, where low-fidelity rollouts guide efficient retrieval of cached high-fidelity data for challenging scenarios. The FAST tokenizer training pipeline will be modified to sample batches from this cache using a learned retrieval model that prioritizes data maximizing learning progress, decoupling simulation from training.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable infrastructure for generating and utilizing ultra-large-scale robot training datasets, directly enabling the exploration of embodiment scaling laws. It will yield a new state-of-the-art, open-world VLA tokenizer trained on unprecedented data diversity, and a general methodology for decoupling data generation from model training in robotics.
proposals.novelty: While distributed simulation exists, the novel adaptation of hierarchical caching strategies from computational cosmology to create a *retrieval-optimized* simulation data lake for robot learning is unexplored. This moves beyond simple replay buffers to an intelligent, multi-fidelity data ecosystem for foundation model training.

proposals.title: PIVOT-Task: Hybrid Zero-Shot/Few-Shot Robot Control via Iterative Visual Prompting and Targeted Fine-Tuning
proposals.motivation: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models like π0.5 enable zero-shot instruction following but struggle with tasks requiring precise, dexterous, or domain-specific skills not well-represented in their broad pre-training data. Conversely, collecting massive robot datasets for every new task is infeasible. A method is needed to efficiently adapt powerful, generalist VLAs to specific, challenging tasks with minimal in-domain data.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating the iterative visual prompting mechanism of the PIVOT framework with a minimal, targeted fine-tuning stage on a small set of task-specific demonstrations, we can achieve a hybrid control paradigm that retains the robust zero-shot generalization of the base VLA while acquiring specialized skills for precise tasks.
proposals.method: We will build upon the PIVOT framework, which uses iterative visual prompting to refine a VLA's (e.g., π0.5) action predictions. We will augment this with a two-phase adaptation: 1) A lightweight, parameter-efficient fine-tuning stage (e.g., using LoRA or adapter modules) on a small curated dataset (<50 demonstrations) of the target task (e.g., peg insertion, cable routing). 2) The fine-tuned model will then be deployed within the PIVOT loop, where iterative visual feedback from the robot further corrects residual errors. We will experiment with fine-tuning only the action tokenizer (inspired by FAST) versus the visual-language-action pathway.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical recipe for efficiently specializing generalist robot policies to precise tasks, significantly reducing the data burden. It will yield a measurable benchmark comparing hybrid adaptation against pure zero-shot and pure few-shot baselines on a suite of dexterous manipulation tasks. The resulting PIVOT-Task framework will demonstrate how to blend large-scale pre-training with targeted, real-world skill acquisition.
proposals.novelty: While PIVOT introduced iterative visual prompting for zero-shot correction and others have fine-tuned VLAs, the non-obvious integration lies in using minimal fine-tuning to 'prime' the model for a task domain, then employing PIVOT's closed-loop prompting to handle instance-specific variations and errors, creating a synergistic hybrid.

proposals.title: PIVOT-LH: Scaling Iterative Vision-Language-Action Refinement for Long-Horizon Robotic Tasks
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language-action models (VLAs) like π0.5 and methods like FAST enable impressive single-task generalization but struggle with complex, long-horizon tasks requiring sequential reasoning and error recovery. Scaling robot learning to such intricate, multi-step goals (e.g., "clean the kitchen") is a critical open challenge for real-world utility. An iterative refinement paradigm, as in PIVOT, offers a promising path but has not been systematically extended to manage long action sequences and dynamic sub-goal planning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical, memory-augmented iterative refinement framework, built upon efficient action tokenization, can enable VLAs to decompose, execute, and recover within long-horizon tasks, significantly outperforming monolithic policy execution.
proposals.method: We will extend the PIVOT framework with a two-level hierarchy. A high-level planner, using a fine-tuned large language model, will decompose natural language instructions into a sequence of actionable sub-goals described in language. A low-level VLA policy (based on π0.5 architecture with FAST tokenization) will execute each sub-goal, with its iterative refinement loop activated per sub-step. A working memory module will track progress and condition both levels, enabling recovery from drift or failure by re-planning subsequent sub-goals.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a scalable architecture for long-horizon VLA control, 2) a benchmark for evaluating compositional task performance in simulation and on real robots, and 3) empirical analysis of failure modes and recovery mechanisms in sequential decision-making. It directly advances the goal of open-world generalization for embodied agents.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating the iterative low-level refinement of PIVOT with a learned, memory-driven high-level planner specifically for VLAs, moving beyond single-step correction to manage compositional task structure—a key gap between current VLAs and embodied reasoning.

proposals.title: Action-Conditioned Visual State Estimation: Using Executed Robot Actions as Prompts for Vision-Language Models
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language-action (VLA) models, like π0.5, primarily use language to condition action selection. However, grounding the robot's understanding of the world state, especially changes caused by its own actions, remains a challenge. This limits the robot's ability to verify task success, detect failures, and reason about action effects in open-world settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a robot's executed action sequence can serve as an effective visual prompt for a VLM, enabling it to more accurately and efficiently infer the resulting world state changes compared to visual-only or language-only conditioning.
proposals.method: We will extend a VLA model architecture (e.g., based on FAST tokenization) with a dual-pathway inference module. Given a pre-action image and a sequence of executed action tokens, we will train a transformer-based adapter to generate a fused 'action-visual prompt.' This prompt will condition a frozen, pre-trained VLM (e.g., CLIP or a VLA encoder) to answer queries about post-action scene properties, object states, and task completion. We will train and evaluate on diverse robotic manipulation datasets, benchmarking against baselines that use only post-action images or natural language descriptions of the action.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel method for action-aware visual state estimation, providing robots with a learned model of action effects. It will advance VLA models beyond action prediction to include state inference, a key component for closed-loop control and long-horizon reasoning. We will release a benchmark for evaluating action-conditioned scene understanding.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses actions to predict outcomes or uses language to prompt VLMs, the explicit use of low-level executed action tokens as a direct conditioning signal for a VLM's perceptual understanding is novel. This inverts the typical VLA direction, using actions to query perception rather than perception to query actions.

proposals.title: Semantic Abstraction for Generalization: Testing the Sufficiency of Non-Geometric Priors in Vision-Language-Action Models
proposals.motivation: Current VLAs often rely on explicit geometric or 3D priors for policy learning, which can be brittle to domain shifts and require precise, costly data. As models scale, a key open question is whether high-level semantic reasoning—understanding object affordances, task structure, and abstract relationships—can provide a more robust and generalizable foundation for embodied control, potentially simplifying the learning problem.
proposals.hypothesis: For a broad set of manipulation and navigation tasks, a VLA policy trained with strong semantic priors but explicitly deprived of low-level geometric representations (e.g., depth, 3D coordinates, explicit spatial maps) can match or exceed the generalization performance of geometry-aware baselines in novel visual and physical environments.
proposals.method: We will extend the π0.5/FAST architecture, replacing explicit geometric tokenization streams with enhanced semantic abstraction modules. These will leverage large pre-trained vision-language models (e.g., CLIP, DINOv2) to extract dense, task-relevant feature fields and object-centric representations. Policy learning, via action token prediction, will be conditioned solely on these semantic embeddings and language instructions. We will design a systematic benchmark comparing this 'Semantic-VLA' against geometry-based VLAs on Meta-World and tailored real-robot tabletop suites, with controlled variations in object appearance, layout, and lighting.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous empirical test of the 'geometric grounding' assumption in robot learning. It will contribute a new model architecture that prioritizes semantic abstraction and a diagnostic benchmark to disentangle the roles of geometric vs. semantic reasoning in generalization. The findings could redefine the necessary inductive biases for scalable embodied AI.
proposals.novelty: While prior work incorporates geometry, we explicitly ablate it to test if semantics are sufficient—a counter-intuitive but crucial direction given scaling trends. This directly challenges a core tenet of classical robotics within a modern VLA framework.

proposals.title: Open-World Action Tokenization from Unstructured Demonstrations (OWL-ACT)
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language-action (VLA) models rely heavily on synthetic or heavily curated robot data, which is expensive to scale and fails to capture the diversity of real-world physical interactions. This bottleneck limits the generalization and robustness of learned policies. Learning directly from the vast, unstructured corpus of real-world human videos and demonstrations offers a path to scalable, diverse, and physically-grounded skill acquisition.
proposals.hypothesis: By jointly learning a temporally-aware action tokenizer and a policy model from unstructured real-world videos, we can achieve more robust and generalizable robot control than models trained solely on synthetic robot trajectories.
proposals.method: We will extend the FAST action tokenization framework to process long, unstructured video sequences (e.g., Ego4D, Something-Something). A key innovation is a contrastive objective that aligns video segments with concurrent narration or instruction text, while a temporal transformer distills these into discrete, reusable action tokens. These tokens will then be used to train a VLA policy (inspired by π0.5 architecture) on a smaller set of paired robot demonstrations, bootstrapping the action space from real-world priors. We will employ masked modeling and next-token prediction losses for pre-training.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a method to scalably pre-train action representations from real-world data, reducing dependency on synthetic data. It will demonstrate improved zero-shot generalization and sample efficiency on a suite of open-world manipulation tasks. The resulting model and learned action tokenizer will be released as a community resource.
proposals.novelty: While prior work tokenizes actions from robot data, our method learns the action vocabulary directly from unstructured human videos, decoupling action representation learning from robot-specific kinematics. This contrasts with concurrent VLA models that are fundamentally limited by their curated action spaces.

proposals.title: ECoT-Real: Bridging the Sim-to-Real Gap for Embodied Chain-of-Thought with Interactive Data
proposals.motivation: Current Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs) like π0.5 and methods like FAST rely heavily on synthetic data (e.g., from simulators or scripted interactions) for training, which creates a 'grounding gap' where policies fail on the distribution shift and physical complexities of real-world deployment. To achieve robust, generalizable robot intelligence, models must be trained on data that captures the full sensory-motor contingencies and failure modes of physical interaction. This project addresses the critical need to scalably incorporate real-world interactive data into the training of embodied reasoning models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that strategically interleaving large-scale synthetic data with targeted, autonomously collected real-world interaction data during the training of an Embodied Chain-of-Thought (ECoT) model will significantly improve its grounding, leading to superior real-world task success rates and robustness compared to models trained solely on synthetic data.
proposals.method: We will extend the π0.5/FAST architecture, using action tokenization for efficient real-world data ingestion. The method involves a two-phase training pipeline: 1) Initial pre-training on massive synthetic (simulated and scripted) datasets to establish broad task and language knowledge. 2) A focused co-training phase where the model engages in targeted real-world interaction on a diverse fleet of robots, collecting on-policy failure and success trajectories. These real trajectories, annotated with automatically generated ECoT reasoning traces via a frozen large language model, will be mixed into the training batch. We will employ a data-balancing scheduler, prioritizing real data in the loss based on its estimated grounding error.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new VLA model, ECoT-Real, with demonstrably stronger real-world performance, and a scalable recipe for integrating real robot data into large-scale embodied AI training. It will provide the community with quantitative analysis of the sim-to-real transfer benefits of interactive data for high-level reasoning and a new dataset of real-world ECoT-annotated robot interactions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses real data for low-level skills or fine-tuning, our approach is novel in its direct, large-scale co-training of a high-level reasoning VLA on a hybrid stream of synthetic reasoning data and autonomously collected physical interaction data, explicitly targeting the grounding of the model's internal 'chain of thought' in physical dynamics.

proposals.title: ECoT-WM: Scaling Embodied Chain-of-Thought Reasoning with Learned World Models for Long-Horizon Planning
proposals.motivation: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models like π0.5 and methods like FAST enable robots to follow diverse instructions, but they struggle with long-horizon tasks requiring multi-step reasoning and prediction. Current VLAs are largely reactive, lacking the internal simulation capacity to plan over extended sequences or anticipate the consequences of actions in novel environments. Integrating the reasoning capabilities of VLAs with the predictive power of world models is a critical next step for scalable embodied intelligence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a VLA's embodied chain-of-thought (ECoT) reasoning module with a learned, latent world model will enable more sample-efficient learning and robust long-horizon planning by allowing the agent to internally simulate and evaluate potential action sequences before execution.
proposals.method: We will extend the π0.5 architecture by adding a latent world model (e.g., based on RSSM or Transformer-based dynamics) trained on the robot's interaction data. The VLA's ECoT module will generate candidate action sequences, which the world model will roll out in its latent space to predict outcomes. A learned value function will score these imagined trajectories, and the highest-scoring plan will be executed. We will use the efficient action tokenization from FAST and train the system end-to-end on a mixture of real robot data and simulated long-horizon tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a unified VLA-World Model architecture capable of internal simulation for planning. It will demonstrate improved sample efficiency and success rates on long-horizon, multi-stage manipulation and navigation benchmarks, providing a blueprint for scaling embodied reasoning through model-based imagination.
proposals.novelty: While world models and VLAs have been developed separately, their tight integration for *embodied* chain-of-thought reasoning is novel. This moves beyond using world models purely for state prediction or VLAs for direct policy learning, instead using the world model as a reasoning substrate for the VLA's planned action sequences.

proposals.title: Episodic Memory-Augmented VLAs for Long-Horizon, Multi-Session Task Completion
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language-action (VLA) models, including π0.5, excel at short-horizon, single-session tasks but struggle with tasks that require persistence over longer timeframes or across multiple deployment sessions. This limits their practical utility in real-world settings like home assistance, where tasks like 'organize the garage' or 'monitor and water all plants' unfold over days. Scaling to such tasks is a critical next step for embodied AI.
proposals.hypothesis: Augmenting a VLA policy with a structured, learnable episodic memory module will enable efficient retention and recall of task-relevant information across long time horizons and multiple sessions, significantly improving success rates on such tasks without catastrophic forgetting.
proposals.method: We will extend the π0.5 VLA architecture with a differentiable, hierarchical episodic memory. This memory will use a transformer-based key-value store, where keys are embeddings of language subgoals and visual observations, and values store corresponding action histories and environmental state summaries. A learned gating mechanism, inspired by Fast Weight Programmers, will decide what to store and retrieve. The policy will be trained on a curriculum of procedurally generated, multi-session simulation tasks in a domain like VirtualHome, using a combination of imitation learning on expert demonstrations with memory access and reinforcement learning to refine the memory retrieval strategy.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel VLA agent capable of long-horizon, multi-session task execution, a new benchmark suite for evaluating such capabilities, and a deeper understanding of how to integrate persistent memory into end-to-end robot policies. It directly addresses a key limitation in moving VLAs from lab demos to sustained real-world operation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses temporal context windows or simple recurrent networks, our approach explicitly structures memory for high-level task progression and cross-session persistence, integrating it as a learnable component within a modern VLA framework rather than as a separate system.

proposals.title: V-DITTO: Visually-Driven Imitation of Tasks from Temporal Observations
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language-action (VLA) models rely heavily on language for task specification and generalization, which limits their applicability in non-linguistic domains or with non-expert users. A policy that learns directly from raw visual demonstrations, without language grounding, would be more universally accessible and could leverage the vast, untapped corpora of unlabeled video data. This addresses a core challenge in embodied AI: achieving scalable skill acquisition from passive observation, akin to human imitation learning.
proposals.hypothesis: A transformer-based policy, trained via contrastive learning on temporally-aligned video demonstrations and robot proprioception, can learn a generalizable latent skill space that enables zero-shot imitation of novel manipulation tasks from a single visual example, without any language input.
proposals.method: We will extend the VLA architecture from π0.5, replacing the language encoder with a dedicated demonstration encoder. This encoder, a VideoMAE-style transformer, will process third-person demonstration videos. A temporal contrastive objective (inspired by TimeSformer) will align segments of the demonstration embedding trajectory with the corresponding robot action trajectory from a diverse offline dataset of robot teleoperation. The policy will be a diffusion model (as in π0.5) conditioned on the current robot state and the encoded demonstration goal, trained to reconstruct actions. We will use the large-scale, language-free RT-X dataset for pre-training.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a generalist, language-agnostic visuomotor policy capable of one-shot visual imitation. It will demonstrate that dense temporal alignment between observation and action spaces is sufficient for task understanding, challenging the necessity of language as a primary grounding mechanism. The model and benchmark will be released to advance research in demonstration-based robot learning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses language for task modulation or employs behavioral cloning on paired data, our method uniquely focuses on learning a cross-modal embedding between unlabeled third-person video and first-person robot data at scale, enabling generalization to unseen tasks from a single video demo without any intermediate language representation.

proposals.title: Causal Affordance Graphs for Robust Vision-Language-Action Policies
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language-action (VLA) models, like π0.5, learn statistical correlations from large datasets but often fail to generalize to novel object arrangements or physical perturbations, as they lack an understanding of causal object affordances and physical relationships. This limits robust real-world manipulation. Integrating causal inference can enable robots to reason about *why* an action succeeds or fails, moving beyond pattern matching to grounded physical understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly modeling object affordances and their physical dependencies as a causal graph inferred from interaction data, a VLA policy can achieve significantly more robust and generalizable manipulation, especially in the face of novel configurations and interventions on the scene.
proposals.method: We will extend the VLA architecture from π0.5/FAST by adding a causal inference module. This module will use interventional data from robot interactions (e.g., poking, grasping) to learn a Structural Causal Model (SCM) representing object affordances (e.g., *rollable*, *containable*) and their relationships. The SCM will be integrated into the action tokenization process, where the policy's cross-attention layers can query the causal graph to reason about necessary preconditions and action effects before generating low-level actions, enabling counterfactual reasoning for robust planning.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel method for integrating causal graphical models with large-scale VLA policies for robotics, and 2) a new benchmark for evaluating causal robustness in manipulation, measuring performance under systematic physical interventions and novel object compositions. It aims to shift VLA models from learning associative mappings to learning causal world models.
proposals.novelty: While causal inference has been explored in isolation and VLA models are scaling, their integration for grounding language and action in a learned causal model of physical affordances is novel. This moves beyond using causality only for explanation or data augmentation to making it a core, actionable component of the policy.

proposals.title: Dual: Co-Training Predictive World Models and Generalist Policies from Robot Interaction Data
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language-action (VLA) models, like π0.5, learn direct action policies from large datasets but lack explicit internal models of physical dynamics. This limits their ability to reason about long-horizon consequences, perform counterfactual planning, or adapt efficiently to novel objects and environments. Learning a predictive world model from the same interaction data could unlock these capabilities, moving beyond pure behavioral cloning towards more robust and generalizable robot intelligence.
proposals.hypothesis: A dual training objective that jointly learns a predictive world model and a generalist action policy from robot interaction data will yield a system with superior sample efficiency, task generalization, and long-horizon reasoning compared to policy-only VLA models.
proposals.method: We will extend the transformer-based VLA architecture from π0.5/FAST. The model will have two output heads: 1) a policy head predicting discretized actions (using FAST tokenization), and 2) a world model head predicting future visual and language-encoded states (e.g., as patch tokens). We will train on large-scale robot datasets (e.g., Open X-Embodiment) using a combined loss: a behavioral cloning loss on actions and a contrastive or autoregressive prediction loss on future observations. The world model's latent representations will be shared with the policy transformer, enabling the policy to implicitly leverage learned dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate the first large-scale co-training of a generalist world model and policy from diverse robot data. It will provide empirical evidence on whether world models improve VLA policy generalization and enable new capabilities like mental simulation for planning. The resulting 'Dual' model and dataset benchmarks will be released to the community.
proposals.novelty: While world models are studied in narrow domains, their integration and co-training with large-scale, multi-task, vision-language-action policies is unexplored. The novelty lies in the dual-head architecture and training objective designed for heterogeneous, real-world robot interaction data, moving beyond learning policies in isolation.

proposals.title: VLM-Reward: Fine-Tuning Vision-Language Models for Dense, Task-Aligned Reward Generation in Robotics
proposals.motivation: Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) show promise for generating reward functions from language instructions, enabling instruction-following robots. However, pre-trained VLMs, trained on internet-scale data, often produce sparse or misaligned reward signals for specific robot embodiments and tasks, leading to inefficient or unstable policy learning. This work addresses the critical gap between general VLM capabilities and the precise, embodiment-aware reward specification needed for robust real-world robot training.
proposals.hypothesis: Fine-tuning a pre-trained VLM (e.g., RT-2, PaLM-E) on a diverse dataset of robot trajectories annotated with success/failure and dense reward labels will produce a model that generates more temporally informative and embodiment-aligned reward functions, significantly improving the sample efficiency and final performance of policies trained with these rewards.
proposals.method: We will adapt a pre-trained VLM (like the π0.5 architecture) by fine-tuning its visual and language encoders on a dataset of robot (vision, action, language) triples. The model will be trained with a multi-task objective: (1) a contrastive loss aligning successful trajectory segments with their instruction, and (2) a regression loss predicting a dense, per-timestep reward signal derived from ground-truth metrics (e.g., distance to goal). We will leverage efficient fine-tuning techniques (e.g., LoRA) and explore action tokenization methods from FAST to compactly represent robot actions. The fine-tuned 'VLM-Reward' model will then be used to generate rewards for training reinforcement learning policies on novel tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a robust, fine-tuned VLM specifically for robot reward generation, demonstrating improved policy learning efficiency over policies using rewards from off-the-shelf VLMs. It will provide a systematic analysis of how different fine-tuning data regimes (e.g., multi-task vs. single-task robot data) affect reward specificity and generalization, contributing a new benchmark for VLM-based reward learning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses VLMs zero-shot for rewards or for high-level planning, this proposal systematically investigates targeted fine-tuning to bridge the 'embodiment gap' for reward generation. It uniquely combines dense reward prediction with contrastive alignment within a single VLM framework optimized for robotics.

proposals.title: Discrete Action Abstractions for Robust Preference-Based Reward Learning
proposals.motivation: Learning reward functions from human preferences or demonstrations is crucial for scaling robot learning to complex tasks, but current methods are brittle when demonstrations are suboptimal or noisy. Existing approaches, like those in inverse reinforcement learning or direct preference optimization, often overfit to low-level execution noise, conflating it with task intent. This limits data efficiency and generalization, especially when only a few imperfect demonstrations are available.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that compressing continuous state-action trajectories into sequences of discrete latent codes via a VQ-VAE will create a robust abstraction layer that filters execution noise, enabling a reward model trained on these discrete sequences to more accurately capture human intent and preferences from messy demonstrations.
proposals.method: We will first train a VQ-VAE on a diverse dataset of robot trajectories to learn a discrete codebook of action primitives. For preference learning, we will encode trajectory pairs into sequences of these discrete tokens. A transformer-based reward model will then be trained on these token sequences using Bradley-Terry preference models, learning to predict human choices. The policy, potentially a vision-language-action model like π0.5, will be fine-tuned using rewards computed in this discrete latent space via reinforcement learning or behavior cloning.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel pipeline for robust preference-based reward learning that is less sensitive to demonstration quality. It will demonstrate improved sample efficiency and generalization in reward learning from limited, noisy data. The method will provide a principled way to separate task semantics from low-level noise, advancing towards more data-efficient robot learning.
proposals.novelty: While VQ-VAEs are known in generative modeling and discrete representations are used in vision-language-action models (e.g., for action tokenization), their application as a dedicated noise-filtering abstraction layer for preference and reward learning from trajectories is novel. This integrates discrete representation learning directly into the reward learning pipeline to explicitly address robustness.

proposals.title: Dense Reward Interpolation via Diffusion Models for Vision-Language-Guided Policy Learning
proposals.motivation: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) offer a scalable source of high-level, semantic feedback for robot learning, but their judgments are often sparse and temporally abstract, making direct policy optimization inefficient. Dense, shaped rewards are crucial for sample-efficient learning, yet manually designing them for every task is impractical. This creates a critical gap between the rich semantic understanding of VLMs and the dense, temporally smooth signals needed for training robust policies.
proposals.hypothesis: A diffusion model, trained on the temporal structure of dense reward signals from successful demonstrations, can effectively interpolate sparse VLM feedback into a complete, plausible reward function, thereby enabling efficient policy learning from abstract language supervision.
proposals.method: We will train a conditional diffusion model on time-aligned, dense reward sequences (e.g., from R3M or DINOv2 features) extracted from expert robot trajectories. The model will learn to reverse a noise process to generate realistic reward progressions. For a new task, a VLM (like a fine-tuned CLIP or BLIP) will provide sparse, binary or scalar feedback at key timesteps. This sparse signal will serve as conditioning for the pre-trained diffusion model, which will perform iterative denoising to generate a full, dense reward trajectory. This interpolated reward will then be used to train a policy via offline RL (e.g., IQL or Diffusion Policy) or online fine-tuning.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled method to densify sparse semantic feedback, bridging VLM-based evaluation and practical policy optimization. It will demonstrate improved sample efficiency and task success rates in vision-language-action settings compared to using raw VLM scores. The resulting framework, 'Reward Diffusion', will be a new tool for automating reward design in embodied AI.
proposals.novelty: While diffusion models are used for trajectory generation and data imputation, their application as a *reward function interpolator*, specifically to translate sparse VLM judgments into dense training signals, is a novel concept. This adapts techniques from geophysical data reconstruction to a core robotics learning problem.

proposals.title: RoboCLIP-MAML: One-Shot Policy Adaptation via Meta-Learning from a Vision-Language-Action Prior
proposals.motivation: Current robot learning struggles with sample efficiency, making it impractical to train new policies for every novel task. While foundation models like RoboCLIP (or VLA models such as π0.5) provide a strong, general-purpose visuomotor prior, they lack the ability to specialize precisely from minimal data. Enabling robots to adapt to new tasks from a single demonstration is a critical step toward practical, generalist robots.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learning algorithm, trained to efficiently update the parameters of a pre-trained RoboCLIP-style policy network, can leverage the model's rich prior knowledge to achieve one-shot adaptation to unseen tasks, outperforming both fine-tuning the prior from scratch and meta-learning without such a prior.
proposals.method: We will instantiate a RoboCLIP-style Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model as a frozen, general-purpose policy network. A meta-learner, based on a recurrent or transformer context encoder and an optimization-based meta-RL algorithm like MAML or Reptile, will be trained. Given a single demonstration (video or state-action sequence), the meta-learner will predict a small set of task-specific parameters or gradients that, when applied to the VLA model's final layers, produce an adapted policy. Training will occur across a large, procedurally generated distribution of simulated manipulation tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a concrete method for one-shot robotic policy adaptation that tightly integrates foundation model priors with meta-RL. It will provide empirical evidence on how the scale and nature of the prior affect meta-learning efficiency, contributing to an understanding of 'embodiment scaling laws' for adaptation.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is framing the VLA model not as an end policy but as a fixed, rich parameter space for meta-learning, analogous to a general immune repertoire. This differs from prior work that either fine-tunes foundation models or uses meta-RL from scratch, combining them in a new, synergistic architecture.

proposals.title: Meta-RoboCLIP: One-Shot Policy Adaptation via Meta-Learned Visual-Language Priors
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language-action (VLA) models like RoboCLIP and π0.5 enable open-world generalization by grounding policies in language and vision, but they still require significant online fine-tuning or demonstrations to adapt to new, specific tasks. Meta-reinforcement learning (meta-RL) excels at rapid adaptation from limited experience but typically relies on dense, handcrafted reward functions and struggles with the high-dimensional, multimodal observations of real robots. Combining these paradigms can unlock efficient, one-shot adaptation for physical robots.
proposals.hypothesis: By meta-learning the adaptation process over a distribution of tasks defined by language and visual demonstrations, a VLA model can acquire a prior that enables effective policy adaptation from a single visual demonstration, without task-specific reward engineering.
proposals.method: We will extend the RoboCLIP/π0.5 architecture, where a transformer maps visual observations and language goals to actions or action tokens. We will formulate a meta-RL problem where each task is defined by a language instruction and a single demonstration video. The model's initial parameters will be meta-trained using an algorithm like MAML or Reptile across a diverse dataset of robotic tasks. The inner-loop adaptation will consist of a few gradient steps (or a forward pass through a learned adapter network) conditioned on the embedding of the provided demonstration, rapidly specializing the policy. Training will leverage large-scale simulated task distributions and transfer to real robots via existing sim-to-real techniques.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a unified framework that achieves one-shot visual imitation for robots by combining the generalization of VLAs with the adaptation speed of meta-RL. It will demonstrate significant reductions in the data and time needed for policy adaptation on novel tasks compared to fine-tuning baseline VLAs or training meta-RL from scratch.
proposals.novelty: While meta-RL and VLAs have been developed in parallel, their integration for one-shot adaptation from a visual demonstration is unexplored. The key novelty is using the demonstration as the sole adaptation signal within a meta-learned VLA policy, eliminating the need for a per-task reward function.

proposals.title: Coordinated π0.5: One-Shot Multi-Agent Reward Generation from Team Demonstrations
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 vision-language-action (VLA) models like π0.5 enable one-shot reward generation for single robots, but scaling to multi-agent collaborative tasks remains a critical open challenge. Real-world applications like furniture assembly or collaborative transport require coordinating multiple robots from a single, high-level human instruction, a problem where existing methods struggle to generate structured, role-specific rewards. This work aims to bridge the gap between scalable VLA policies and the complexities of multi-robot coordination.
proposals.hypothesis: A VLA model can be extended to generate structured, factorized reward functions for multiple agents from a single team demonstration and language instruction, enabling zero-shot generalization to novel multi-agent tasks without per-agent demonstration.
proposals.method: We will extend the π0.5 architecture by integrating a factorized action tokenization head, inspired by FAST, to output separate action or goal sequences for each robot. The model will be trained on a new dataset of simulated multi-agent collaborative tasks (e.g., from Meta-World or custom environments) with paired video demonstrations, language instructions, and per-agent action labels. A key technical innovation is a contrastive learning objective that aligns the global team instruction with individual agent sub-goals, ensuring coordinated behavior emerges from the shared world model.
proposals.contribution: This will produce the first VLA model capable of one-shot reward and policy generation for multi-robot teams, directly extending the 'one-shot' paradigm to collaborative settings. We will contribute a new benchmark for multi-agent VLA evaluation and demonstrate improved sample efficiency and generalization over training separate single-agent models or using centralized controllers.
proposals.novelty: While prior work scales VLA models to larger single robots or datasets, we uniquely scale the 'one-shot reward generation' capability along the axis of multi-agent coordination. The method's novelty lies in using a single team demonstration to automatically decompose a task into synchronized, role-specific rewards without explicit programming of coordination structures.

proposals.title: RoboCLIP-Safe: Real-Time Anomaly Detection for Robust Embedded Reward Computation
proposals.motivation: Deploying robots in unstructured environments requires reward functions robust to visual novelty and domain shift.
