Current vision-language-action models (VLAs) like RoboCLIP or π0.5 are vulnerable to out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs, which can cause catastrophic reward mis-specification and unsafe behavior, especially on low-compute platforms where large models cannot run. A method to detect and mitigate these failures in real-time is critical for safe, long-horizon autonomy.
proposals.hypothesis: A lightweight anomaly detector, co-trained with a distilled visual-language encoder for reward computation, can reliably identify OOD visual states in real-time on embedded hardware, enabling safe fallback behaviors without compromising in-distribution task performance.
proposals.method: We will distill a RoboCLIP-style VLA into a tiny Transformer for embedded reward computation. Concurrently, we will train a lightweight, memory-augmented autoencoder (e.g., based on a compact ConvNet) on the robot's training data to model the visual encoder's latent space. At runtime, the reconstruction error from this detector will signal OOD states. We will integrate this signal into a hierarchical policy, triggering a pre-trained safe policy or help-request when anomalies exceed a threshold, building on prior work in graceful degradation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a system for robust, real-time reward computation on resource-constrained robots. It will provide a concrete framework for safe VLA-based policy execution under visual uncertainty and empirical benchmarks for OOD detection in embodied vision-language models.
proposals.novelty: The tight co-design of a distilled reward VLA and a concurrent, efficient anomaly detector for embedded execution is novel. Unlike prior offline OOD detection, this enables real-time safety arbitration within the robot's control loop, directly addressing the deployment gap for VLAs.

proposals.title: Lifelong Reward Learning from Continuous Human Video Streams
proposals.motivation: Current robot learning from human videos is limited to curated, finite datasets, which restricts adaptation to new tasks and environments. For robots to operate long-term in human spaces, they must learn continuously from the vast, unstructured video data available online (e.g., YouTube, Egocentric streams). This requires methods to scalably generate reward functions and policy updates from a never-ending, non-stationary stream of visual demonstrations.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting and scaling vision-language-action (VLA) model training to process continuous video streams online, we can learn a continually improving reward function that enables lifelong policy adaptation for robotic manipulation.
proposals.method: We will extend the π0.5 VLA model architecture with a streaming, memory-efficient training loop. Inspired by FAST tokenization, we will develop a temporally-aware action tokenizer for long video sequences. A contrastive learning objective will align video segments with language-derived intent, auto-generating reward signals. We will employ experience replay and regularization techniques to mitigate catastrophic forgetting, enabling the model to learn incrementally from a simulated stream of egocentric and third-person human activity videos.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a framework for lifelong reward learning from video, moving beyond static datasets. It will demonstrate how scaling to continuous data improves the robustness and task generality of learned robotic policies, providing a key step towards robots that learn from the open world in real-time.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses fixed video datasets for offline reward labeling, our proposal focuses on the online, continual learning setting for reward generation, adapting VLA models to non-stationary data streams without manual curation or reset.

proposals.title: Inverse Policy Visualization: Interpreting Robot Policies via Seismic-Inversion-Inspired Visual Reconstruction
proposals.motivation: As robot policies scale in complexity, especially with vision-language-action models, they become opaque black boxes. This lack of interpretability hinders debugging, safety verification, and trust. Current explanation methods like saliency maps or feature visualization are low-level and do not translate policy logic into the human-intuitive space of visual task demonstrations.
proposals.hypothesis: The low-dimensional action/state trajectory of a trained policy can be inverted by a physics-constrained Vision-Language Model (VLM) to reconstruct a high-level, human-interpretable visual narrative of the policy's intended task, analogous to how seismic data is inverted to visualize subsurface geology.
proposals.method: We will treat a robot's executed state-action trajectory as observed 'wave' data. A VLM (e.g., a fine-tuned variant of RT-2 or a π0.5-like model) will act as the inversion engine, conditioned on this trajectory and a physics-based world model (e.g., MuJoCo or NVIDIA Isaac Gym dynamics). The VLM will be trained via contrastive learning to generate the most plausible sequence of video frames and descriptive captions (a 'visual demonstration') that is both physically consistent with the world model and logically predictive of the observed policy behavior. We will adapt iterative optimization techniques from seismic inversion (e.g., gradient-based model updating) to refine the VLM's output.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel paradigm for explaining robot policies by translating them into human-understandable visual demonstrations. It will contribute a benchmark for policy interpretability based on reconstruction fidelity and human evaluation, and a method to audit and refine policies by comparing their visual narratives to human intent.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the conceptual and methodological transfer of the geophysical inverse problem to policy interpretation, moving beyond attribution-based explanations to generative, physics-grounded visual reconstruction of a policy's internal task model.

proposals.title: Dexterous Manipulation via Self-Supervised Skill Discovery and Language-Guided Composition
proposals.motivation: Scaling robot learning to diverse, dexterous manipulation tasks is fundamentally bottlenecked by the cost and specificity of human-provided demonstrations. Pre-2026, methods like VLAs and embodiment scaling laws show promise for generalization but still rely heavily on curated expert data for training. Removing this dependency is crucial for creating truly autonomous, generalist robots that can learn from their own interaction with the world.
proposals.hypothesis: A robot can learn a repertoire of dexterous manipulation skills entirely through self-supervised interaction, and that these skills can be composed via natural language instructions to solve novel tasks without any expert demonstrations.
proposals.method: We will extend the π0.5 VLA architecture, replacing its demonstration-conditioned training with a two-stage, demonstration-free pipeline. First, we will employ unsupervised skill discovery (e.g., DIAYN or variational empowerment maximization) on offline robot interaction videos to learn a library of reusable, temporally-extended action primitives (tokenized via methods like FAST). Second, we will train a language-conditioned skill composer using web-scale video-text data (e.g., Ego4D, Something-Something) to map language instructions to sequences of these discovered skill tokens, enabling zero-shot task execution.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a complete framework for learning dexterous manipulation policies without human demonstrations. It will empirically demonstrate the feasibility of scaling skill acquisition through self-supervised interaction and language-guided composition, pushing towards more autonomous and generalist robot learning. The resulting model and skill library will be a benchmark for future demonstration-free research.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses demonstrations for VLAs or explores skill discovery in isolation, this proposal uniquely combines large-scale, self-supervised skill learning from robot proprioception/vision with language grounding from human video datasets, entirely circumventing the need for robot-task-specific human demonstrations.

proposals.title: π-RL: Bootstrapping Vision-Language-Action Policies with Imitation Priors for Sample-Efficient Reinforcement Learning
proposals.motivation: Large-scale imitation learning (IL) on diverse robot datasets, as in π0.5, produces policies with broad open-world generalization but often lacks the precision and robustness required for optimal task performance. Pure reinforcement learning (RL) can achieve this but is notoriously sample-inefficient for real-world robots. Combining these paradigms is a critical path toward both general and high-performing embodied agents.
proposals.hypothesis: A vision-language-action policy pre-trained on large-scale robot data (like π0.5) provides a strong, structured prior that can be efficiently fine-tuned with online RL, leading to superior final performance and significantly improved sample efficiency compared to training from scratch or using naive behavioral cloning initialization.
proposals.method: We will extend the π0.5 architecture, using its pre-trained weights as the policy network initialization. For RL fine-tuning, we will employ an off-policy actor-critic algorithm like Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) or TD3, chosen for sample efficiency. The key technical innovation is the design of a constrained policy optimization objective that penalizes deviation from the IL prior (e.g., via a KL-divergence penalty) to prevent catastrophic forgetting of useful behaviors while allowing exploration for reward maximization. This will be tested on a suite of simulated and real-robot manipulation tasks with sparse and dense reward signals.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a unified framework for combining large-scale IL and RL in robot learning, demonstrating substantial gains in sample efficiency and asymptotic performance. It will provide empirical scaling laws for the utility of IL data as a function of RL fine-tuning steps, contributing to the nascent field of embodiment scaling laws.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has combined IL and RL (e.g., DAPG), our approach uniquely integrates this within a modern, token-based vision-language-action model architecture, leveraging its open-world semantic grounding for more intelligent exploration and constraint during RL. The focus is on scaling the prior's quality and diversity, as enabled by models like π0.5, to fundamentally alter the RL optimization landscape.

proposals.title: PromptCraft: Generating Grounded Task Specifications for Vision-Language-Action Policies via LLM Prompting
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language-action (VLA) models like π0.5 rely on manually curated, often templated, natural language instructions for task specification, which limits their scalability and adaptability to open-world scenarios. Enabling robots to interpret and act upon diverse, free-form user requests is a core challenge for real-world deployment. This work addresses the critical bottleneck of automatically generating actionable, contextually grounded task descriptions from high-level human intent.
proposals.hypothesis: Large language models (LLMs), when prompted with environmental context and affordance constraints, can generate structured, executable task descriptions that enable robust policy execution by a pre-trained VLA model, significantly improving task success rates on novel, user-defined instructions compared to direct instruction following.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage system. First, a perception module (e.g., a pre-trained vision encoder) will extract a symbolic scene graph or object-centric summary of the environment. This context, along with a high-level user command (e.g., 'tidy up the table'), will be formatted into a structured prompt for a frozen LLM (e.g., GPT-4). The LLM will be prompted to output a sequence of sub-task descriptions in a constrained language format compatible with the input space of a VLA policy like π0.5. We will fine-tune the VLA policy's language adapter on this generated data, employing techniques like action tokenization from FAST for efficiency, and evaluate on a suite of long-horizon manipulation tasks in simulation and on real robots.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel framework for bridging high-level LLM reasoning with low-level VLA policy control, 2) a method for generating scalable, grounded training data for task specification, and 3) empirical evidence on the effectiveness of LLM-based task decomposition for improving the generalization of embodied agents to open-world instructions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses LLMs for planning or code generation, our approach uniquely focuses on generating the *natural language task descriptions* that serve as direct input to a pre-trained, end-to-end VLA policy, creating a tight coupling between high-level reasoning and low-level visuomotor control without altering the policy's core architecture.

proposals.title: π-∞: Scaling a Universal Manipulation Policy via Action Tokenization and Embodiment-Aware Data Mixtures
proposals.motivation: Current robot learning struggles with narrow specialization; a policy trained for one task often fails on another. While vision-language-action models like π0.5 show promise for open-world generalization, they are typically trained on limited task diversity. Scaling to thousands of diverse manipulation tasks is the critical next step towards a truly universal, capable robot policy, but it presents massive challenges in data curation, representation learning, and efficient training.
proposals.hypothesis: A single, scalable transformer policy, trained on a massive and strategically composed dataset of thousands of manipulation tasks using efficient action tokenization, will exhibit emergent, positive cross-task generalization, outperforming both specialized policies and smaller-scale generalist models.
proposals.method: We will extend the π0.5 architecture, leveraging the FAST method for efficient, discrete action tokenization to handle diverse robot embodiments and action spaces. We will construct a 'Manipulation-10K' dataset by algorithmically mixing existing sources (e.g., RT-X, Open X-Embodiment, simulated benchmarks) with a focus on embodiment-aware balancing, inspired by scaling law principles from locomotion. Training will employ large-scale distributed RL and imitation learning, treating diverse task instructions and visual observations as inputs to the vision-language backbone and the tokenized actions as targets.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) the largest publicly available universal manipulation policy, 2) a systematic study of scaling laws for manipulation diversity (parallel to embodiment scaling in locomotion), and 3) a new benchmark for evaluating cross-task and cross-embodiment generalization in a single model.
proposals.novelty: While prior work scales model size or single-task data, we uniquely scale *task diversity* to an unprecedented level (thousands) within a single policy. The key novelty is the co-design of the scalable action representation (FAST) with a data mixture strategy explicitly optimized for multi-task, multi-embodiment generalization, moving beyond simple aggregation.

proposals.title: Dual-Agent Coaching: A Vision-Language-Action Model for Mixed-Initiative Human-Robot Skill Transfer
proposals.motivation: While large vision-language-action (VLA) models like π0.5 enable robots to perform diverse tasks from instructions, they are typically one-way systems where the human commands the robot. This misses a critical opportunity for robots to act as proactive coaches, transferring embodied skills back to human operators in complex, high-stakes domains like surgery, manufacturing, or rehabilitation. A mixed-initiative system where a robot can both execute a policy and tutor a human remains an underexplored frontier for human-robot collaboration.
proposals.hypothesis: A VLA model, trained on both robot execution data and paired human demonstration data, can be adapted into a dual-agent policy that not only performs tasks but also generates actionable, context-aware feedback to guide and improve human performance on the same tasks.
proposals.method: We will extend the π0.5 VLA architecture, using its efficient action tokenization (FAST) for robot control. We will co-train the model on datasets containing (state, robot action) pairs and (state, human action, suboptimality metric) triplets. A separate, lightweight critic module will be trained to assess human action quality relative to the robot's policy. For coaching, the model will use its vision-language grounding to generate natural language corrections (e.g., "rotate your wrist 15 degrees clockwise") or visual overlays when it detects a significant deviation from an optimal trajectory, creating a closed-loop mixed-initiative system.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel dual-agent VLA model framework capable of both execution and coaching, 2) a methodology for training such models on paired human-robot data, and 3) empirical validation in simulated and real-world tabletop manipulation tasks, measuring human learning curves with and without robotic coaching.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the typical human-in-the-loop paradigm; instead of the human providing corrections to the robot, the trained robot policy provides corrective feedback to the human, creating a bidirectional skill-transfer loop. While prior work uses VLAs for robot teleoperation or learning from humans, using them to tutor humans is a novel direction.

proposals.title: JEDI: Joint End-to-End Diffusion for Hierarchical Vision-Language-Action Policies
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language-action (VLA) models, such as π0.5, rely on pre-trained low-level skills or action tokenizers (e.g., from FAST) to ground language in robot control. This creates a bottleneck for learning novel tasks that require new low-level primitives not captured by the pre-trained policy. A method to learn hierarchical control from scratch, without this dependency, is crucial for true open-world generalization to unseen tasks and embodiments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical diffusion model, trained end-to-end on paired language, vision, and proprioception, can jointly discover effective low-level action abstractions and high-level task plans, outperforming methods that freeze a pre-trained low-level policy.
proposals.method: We propose a two-level hierarchical diffusion architecture. The high-level planner is a latent diffusion model that, conditioned on language and image goals, generates a sequence of sub-goal embeddings in a learned latent space. The low-level controller is a conditional diffusion policy that takes the current sub-goal embedding and proprioceptive state to output robot actions. The entire model is trained end-to-end on diverse robotic teleoperation datasets using a reconstruction loss on the action sequence, encouraging the latent sub-goal space to be both predictable and actionable.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel end-to-end trainable architecture for hierarchical VLAs that does not require a fixed action vocabulary or pre-trained policy, 2) empirical evidence on the scalability and sample efficiency of such joint training compared to staged approaches, and 3) a new benchmark for evaluating hierarchical policy learning on tasks requiring novel low-level behaviors.
proposals.novelty: While prior work like π0.5 and FAST decouple high-level planning from fixed low-level skills, JEDI's core novelty is the fully joint, end-to-end optimization of both levels via hierarchical diffusion, enabling the discovery of task-optimal primitive actions during training.

proposals.title: VLaP: A Vision-Language-Action Planner for Hierarchical Task Decomposition in Open-World Robotics
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language-action (VLA) models like π0.5 can execute low-level skills but struggle with long-horizon tasks requiring complex subgoal planning. While large language models (LLMs) excel at high-level reasoning, they lack the visual grounding necessary for generating feasible, context-aware subgoals in novel environments. Bridging this gap is crucial for scalable, general-purpose robots that can follow complex natural language instructions.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical planner that uses an LLM for abstract task reasoning, guided and grounded by a visual subgoal proposal network, will enable more robust and generalizable long-horizon task execution than monolithic VLA policies or pure LLM planners.
proposals.method: We will extend the π0.5 architecture with a hierarchical planning module. A frozen LLM (e.g., GPT-4) will generate a textual subgoal sequence given a high-level instruction. A trainable visual subgoal proposal network, built upon a pre-trained visual encoder (e.g., from π0.5), will ground each textual subgoal into a concrete visual representation (e.g., a goal image or feature) by conditioning on the current robot observation. The π0.5 low-level policy will then be conditioned on these visual subgoals to produce actions, using the efficient action tokenization from FAST. The visual proposal network will be trained via imitation learning on demonstration datasets, with a loss that aligns its output with the visual states preceding successful skill execution.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, modular architecture for hierarchical VLA planning that cleanly separates high-level reasoning from visual grounding and low-level control. It will demonstrate improved performance on long-horizon, open-world tasks compared to flat VLA models. The project will also provide insights into how to effectively interface and ground frozen LLMs within embodied action loops.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior LLM-for-robotics work that uses LLMs to output code or direct action sequences, our method uses the LLM solely for abstract reasoning, with a dedicated, trainable visual network for grounding. This addresses the LLM's lack of visual context while maintaining its planning strength, a non-obvious integration given the trend towards end-to-end VLAs.

proposals.title: Noisy Demonstrations are Sufficient: Scaling Robot Learning with Unfiltered Human Data
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language-action (VLA) models, like π0.5, rely on high-quality, often expert, human demonstrations for policy learning, creating a major bottleneck for scaling. In real-world settings, such as homes, data is inherently noisy, suboptimal, and provided by non-experts. Understanding whether and how policies can learn effectively from this 'in-the-wild' human data is critical for moving beyond curated lab datasets to scalable, generalizable robot learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modern VLA model architectures, when combined with appropriate action tokenization and scaling, are robust to the noise and suboptimality present in unfiltered human demonstrations, and can learn performant policies without expert curation.
proposals.method: We will build upon the π0.5 VLA architecture and the FAST action tokenizer. We will collect a large-scale dataset of non-expert human teleoperation data for diverse mobile manipulation tasks, intentionally including noisy trajectories and failures. We will train policies using this unfiltered dataset and systematically compare their performance to policies trained on a carefully curated expert subset. The core experiment will analyze how performance scales with the quantity of noisy data versus the quality of expert data, testing the embodied scaling law premise in a noisy-data regime.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic study of data quality versus quantity trade-offs for VLA policy learning from human demonstrations. It will establish whether scaling with noisy data is a viable path toward generalist robots and deliver a benchmark dataset of noisy human demonstrations for the community.
proposals.novelty: While prior work assumes or curates for high-quality demonstrations, we directly challenge this necessity, testing the robustness of state-of-the-art VLA models to real-world human imperfection—a non-obvious proposition given the field's focus on optimal demonstrations and reward labels.

proposals.title: Scaling Object Generalization: A Benchmark and Analysis for Vision-Language-Action Policies
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 vision-language-action (VLA) models like π0.5 demonstrate impressive multi-task learning but are typically evaluated on objects and scenes similar to their training data. For real-world robot deployment, policies must handle novel objects with unseen visual attributes, shapes, and affordances. A systematic, quantitative benchmark for object-level generalization is missing, limiting our understanding of VLA model robustness and scaling properties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the generalization gap of VLA policies to novel objects is predictable and scales systematically with the diversity and scale of the training object distribution, following principles akin to embodiment scaling laws.
proposals.method: We will construct a benchmark using existing robot datasets (e.g., Bridge, Open X-Embodiment) by partitioning objects into disjoint training and novel evaluation sets based on visual and semantic embeddings. We will evaluate the generalization of VLA models like π0.5 and FAST-tokenized policies on this benchmark. The core analysis will measure performance decay as a function of the 'distance' of novel objects from the training distribution, using metrics from representation learning. We will also experiment with data augmentation and language-guided feature space regularization to improve generalization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous benchmark for object-level generalization in robot VLA policies, establishing quantitative baselines. It will yield insights into how training data diversity affects robustness, informing future data collection and model design for open-world robotics.
proposals.novelty: While prior work tests generalization to new tasks or environments, we focus specifically on systematic, compositional generalization to novel objects—a critical but under-benchmarked axis. Our approach connects generalization performance to training data scaling laws, moving beyond anecdotal evaluation.

proposals.title: VLA-Annotator: Automating Robot Demonstration Annotation with Vision-Language-Action Models
proposals.motivation: Scaling robot learning to diverse tasks requires vast, well-annotated demonstration datasets, but manual labeling is a prohibitive bottleneck. Recent Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models like π0.5 show promising open-world generalization, suggesting they could understand and decompose complex robot videos. This project investigates whether these models can autonomously generate high-quality, structured annotations (e.g., sub-goal segmentation, object affordance labels, task descriptions) from raw demonstration videos, dramatically reducing the cost of curating robot training data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that large vision-language-action foundation models, when prompted and fine-tuned appropriately, can generate accurate and semantically rich annotations for robot demonstration data, and that policies trained on these auto-labeled demonstrations will achieve performance comparable to those trained on human-labeled data.
proposals.method: We will develop VLA-Annotator, a system built upon a VLA backbone (e.g., extending the π0.5 architecture). First, we will curate a seed dataset of robot videos with human-provided dense annotations (temporal segments, language descriptions). We will then explore two primary techniques: 1) In-context learning with sophisticated prompting chains to guide the VLM to output structured annotation tokens, and 2) Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (e.g., LoRA) of the vision-language modules on the seed data to specialize the model for the annotation task. The output will be tokenized annotations compatible with action tokenization frameworks like FAST for downstream policy training.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) A novel, automated pipeline for generating actionable annotations from unlabeled robot videos, 2) A systematic evaluation of the quality of VLA-generated labels versus human labels for policy learning, and 3) A new, scalable method for bootstrapping large-scale robot demonstration datasets, directly supporting research into embodiment scaling laws.
proposals.novelty: While VLMs have been used for image captioning, applying them specifically to annotate *robot action sequences* for downstream policy training is novel. The key innovation is treating the VLA model not as a policy itself, but as an automated data curator for training other, potentially more sample-efficient, policies.

proposals.title: CORAL: Corrective Online Refinement for Ad-hoc Language-defined Tasks
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language-action (VLA) models can follow instructions for known tasks but struggle with novel, user-defined tasks not seen in training. Enabling robots to learn from real-time, language-based human feedback for entirely new objectives is crucial for real-world deployment where tasks are open-ended. This bridges the gap between pre-trained, broad-capability VLAs and the need for on-the-fly personalization and task specification.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical policy, where a high-level VLA model generates subgoal proposals and a low-level model executes them, can learn novel tasks online by treating a sequence of language corrections as a training curriculum for the high-level planner.
proposals.method: We extend a VLA base model like π0.5 into a two-tier hierarchy. The high-level policy outputs discrete skill or action tokens (inspired by FAST tokenization) given an image and a language goal. A human provides corrective language commands (e.g., 'no, move the cup closer first') on failed attempts. We frame these sequential corrections as a synthetic demonstration trajectory for the novel task and use offline RL (e.g., Conservative Q-Learning) to fine-tune only the high-level policy's output heads on this small, corrective dataset, while freezing the visual and language encoders and the low-level policy.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a method for teaching robots new composite tasks without pre-collected demonstrations, using only language corrections. It will contribute a framework for online, language-driven hierarchical policy adaptation and provide empirical results on the scaling of correction efficiency with model size, linking to embodiment scaling laws.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses corrections for refining known tasks, this proposal uses a sequence of corrections as the sole specification for a *novel* task, treating them as a curriculum to synthesize training data for hierarchical policy adaptation. This combines the generality of large VLAs with the precision of online feedback in a novel learning paradigm.

proposals.title: SCALE: Simulated Corrections for Augmenting Language-Embodied Policies
proposals.motivation: Training vision-language-action (VLA) models for robots requires vast, diverse datasets of (observation, language, action) triples, which are expensive and slow to collect in the real world. While simulation can generate synthetic trajectories, these often lack the rich, corrective language feedback that is crucial for learning robust and generalizable high-level policies. This work addresses the data bottleneck by generating large-scale, instructionally rich simulation data to pre-train VLA models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pre-training a VLA policy's high-level action space (e.g., using discrete action tokens like in FAST or π0.5) on a massive, procedurally generated dataset of simulated trajectories paired with corrective language instructions will significantly improve its sample efficiency and zero-shot generalization when fine-tuned on sparse real-world robot data.
proposals.method: We will build on the π0.5/FAST architecture, using a pre-trained vision-language model to encode observations and generate discrete action tokens. In simulation, we will deploy a 'critic' LLM to automatically generate natural language corrections (e.g., 'move the arm higher before grasping') for suboptimal trajectories produced by an exploratory policy. This creates a scalable loop: (policy rollout → LLM critique → policy update). The resulting dataset of (observation, language correction, action) tuples will be used to pre-train the VLA model before fine-tuning on limited real robot data.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a method for generating unbounded, language-rich robot training data via simulated feedback loops, 2) empirical evidence for 'embodiment scaling' in the language-correction domain, showing improved sample efficiency and generalization in real-world tasks, and 3) an open-source simulated feedback dataset and pre-trained model checkpoint for the community.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses human demonstrations or scripted language, our approach uniquely leverages LLMs as automated 'teachers' in simulation to generate diverse, context-aware corrections at scale, creating a novel pre-training paradigm for VLA policies that is distinct from passive trajectory playback.

proposals.title: Inverse Imitation: A Vision-Language-Action Model for Corrective Human Guidance in Physical Tasks
proposals.motivation: Current human-robot collaboration focuses on robots learning from human demonstrations. However, in complex tasks like assembly or surgery, humans can make errors. A system that can observe and provide corrective language feedback to a human operator would enable safer, more efficient co-working. This inverts the typical imitation learning paradigm, requiring a robot to understand task intent, diagnose sub-optimal actions, and communicate effectively.
proposals.hypothesis: A vision-language-action model (VLAM), trained on paired human action sequences and expert corrective language, can learn to generate actionable, step-specific language corrections that improve human task performance in novel, open-world settings.
proposals.method: We will extend the π0.5 architecture, using its strong open-world VLA foundation. A corrective language dataset will be constructed by recording human task attempts (e.g., from Ego4D or new data), pairing them with expert textual corrections. The model will be trained to map from a history of human-centric visual observations (via an egocentric camera) and the current state to a language correction, using a causal transformer. We will leverage efficient action tokenization from FAST to discretize and represent the human's observed motion, enabling the model to reason about action deviations. Training will employ a masked modeling objective for the correction tokens.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce the novel problem of 'inverse imitation' for human correction. It will yield a new benchmark for human-in-the-loop task evaluation and a functional VLA model capable of generating task-grounded, helpful language. This advances human-robot collaboration from passive observation to active, communicative guidance.
proposals.novelty: While VLAMs like π0.5 generate robot actions from language, we invert this to generate corrective language from observed human actions. This shifts the agent's role from imitator to critic/guide, a largely unexplored direction in embodied AI.

ideas.title: Inverse Policy Visualization via Geophysical Seismic Inversion
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to the inverse problem in geophysical seismic inversion to the robotics domain. In seismology, surface wave measurements are inverted to create visual models of subsurface geology. Analogously, here, the low-dimensional action/state trajectories from a trained, opaque robot policy are treated as the observed 'wave data.' A Vision-Language Model (VLM), constrained by a physics-based world model, acts as the inversion engine. It solves for the most plausible sequence of human-interpretable visual task demonstrations (e.g., a video with descriptive captions) that would logically lead to the policy's observed behavior. This process generates intuitive explanations and visualizations of the policy's decision-making logic, addressing the scaling challenge of interpreting complex, scaled-up policies by translating them into the native demonstration space used for human learning and communication.

ideas.title: Robust Embedded Reward with Real-Time Anomaly Detection for Safe Robot Learning
ideas.description: This research develops a robust, real-time reward computation system for low-compute robot platforms that is resilient to out-of-distribution visual inputs. The core innovation is the tight integration of a distilled, efficient visual-language model (based on RoboCLIP) with a concurrently running lightweight anomaly detector, both designed for embedded execution. The anomaly detector continuously assesses the reliability of the visual encoder's output, enabling the robot to identify when its reward function is likely to be unreliable. This allows the system to gracefully degrade performance by switching to a safe, conservative policy or requesting help, thereby preventing catastrophic failures due to visual novelty or domain shift.

ideas.title: One-Shot Policy Adaptation via Meta-Learning and a Foundation Model Prior
ideas.description: This idea combines meta-learning for faster RL adaptation with a foundation model prior, drawing an analogy from computational immunology. In vaccine design, a general immune system model (a prior) is rapidly specialized via meta-learning to target new pathogens with few examples. Similarly, we propose a two-stage method for robotic policy adaptation. First, RoboCLIP provides a rich, pre-trained prior encoding a broad 'repertoire' of visuomotor skills. Second, a meta-RL algorithm is trained to optimally update the parameters of this RoboCLIP-based policy network. The meta-learner is not learning policies from scratch but is instead learning how to efficiently interpret and integrate the context from a single demonstration to perform minimal, high-impact adjustments to the RoboCLIP prior, enabling one-shot adaptation to new tasks.

ideas.title: Diffusion-Driven Tokenization for Continuous Action-Observation Synthesis
ideas.description: This research develops a joint tokenization scheme where actions and observations are represented as a single, continuous token sequence. A diffusion model is trained to operate directly on this unified token space, using sparse, high-level VLM rewards as conditioning. The model can then generate or infill dense, plausible sequences of intermediate action-observation pairs, effectively synthesizing entire sub-trajectories that are dynamically consistent and goal-directed. This creates a powerful world model for planning that reasons over complete interaction cycles, not isolated decisions.

ideas.title: Robust Preference Learning via Discrete Latent Action Abstractions
ideas.description: This research direction integrates vector quantization from generative models into the reward learning pipeline to improve robustness to imperfect demonstrations. A Vector-Quantized Variational Autoencoder (VQ-VAE) is trained to encode high-dimensional, continuous state-action trajectories into a sequence of discrete latent codes, capturing essential temporal abstractions and discarding noisy, suboptimal motion details. The reward model then operates on these discrete latent sequences, learning to predict human preferences by identifying the underlying intent patterns within the structured codebook space, which is inherently more robust to demonstration noise and ambiguity than raw actions. This enables learning from a single, messy demonstration by separating the core task semantics from execution noise through discrete compression.

ideas.title: Discrete Action Codebooks for Cross-Modal VLAs via FAST
ideas.description: This research fuses vector quantization techniques from generative models with the FAST tokenizer's cross-modal capabilities to create a novel VLA architecture. The core idea is to train a shared, discrete codebook that jointly represents visual observations and executable action primitives, using the FAST framework as the bridge. This unified discrete latent space allows transformer or diffusion-based VLAs to plan and generate action sequences with the same structured, compositional reasoning used for visual understanding. The synergy enables more sample-efficient learning of complex, long-horizon tasks by leveraging the representational efficiency and generalization properties of discrete latent variables across perception and action.

ideas.title: Apply Geophysical Seismic Interpolation via Diffusion for Reward Densification
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from geophysical seismic data interpolation to the problem of reward densification. In seismic imaging, diffusion models are used to generate plausible, dense subsurface data traces from sparse sensor measurements by learning the statistical structure of full surveys. Analogously, we propose to train a diffusion model on dense reward sequences (e.g., from expert trajectories) to learn the temporal and causal structure of successful task completion. This model will then act as a reward interpolator: when a Vision-Language Model (VLM) provides sparse, high-level evaluative feedback (e.g., 'object is now closer'), the diffusion model conditions on these sparse anchors and generates a complete, dense reward function by filling in plausible intermediate progress steps. This transforms sparse, abstract VLM judgments into a smooth, training-ready reward signal for policy optimization.

ideas.title: Scalable, Hierarchical Tokenizer Training via Distributed Simulation Caching
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational cosmology, where training on massive universe simulations is scaled using a hierarchical, distributed caching framework. The analogous solution involves generating a vast parameter space of simulations at different resolutions, caching the outputs, and having training pipelines sample intelligently from this cache. Adapted to the original idea of scaling the FAST+ tokenizer on massive robotic simulation data, this means building a federated data lake that pre-processes and stores sensory-motor trajectories from a massive number of diverse simulated environments at multiple abstraction levels. The tokenizer training no longer runs simulations live but samples from this cache, using low-fidelity runs to guide efficient retrieval of high-fidelity data for challenging scenarios, thereby enabling orders-of-magnitude scaling in training data diversity and volume without proportional computational cost during training.

ideas.title: Causality-Preserving Joint Tokenization for Aligned Multi-Modal Sensorimotor Data
ideas.description: This research designs a tokenization framework for continuous robot sensorimotor data that explicitly maintains the temporal and causal dependencies between heterogeneous modalities like proprioception, touch, and audio. Instead of tokenizing each stream independently, it learns a unified set of token codebooks under a constraint that enforces alignment on a shared latent timeline. The training incorporates a causality-aware loss function that uses known physical priors to ensure the tokenized sequences reflect real-world interaction dynamics, making the resulting tokens suitable for temporal prediction and control tasks where timing is critical.

ideas.title: Adapting Morphological Analysis for Hierarchical Action Tokenization
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from linguistics, where morphological analysis breaks down complex words into morphemes, to the problem of tokenizing hierarchical actions. The analogous solution involves identifying a finite set of primitive action 'morphemes' (like sub-goals, motor primitives, or termination signals) and rules for their composition. This adapted research idea proposes to modify or extend the FAST tokenization framework to not just tokenize flat actions, but to parse and generate sequences of these morpheme tokens, thereby creating a structured, hierarchical action vocabulary. This would allow an agent to efficiently plan and execute temporally extended skills by operating over this compositional token space, improving sample efficiency and generalization in hierarchical reinforcement learning.

ideas.title: Query-Efficient Multi-Agent Coordination Policy Distillation from Demonstration and Counterfactual Feedback
ideas.description: This system learns a robust reward function for multi-agent collaborative tasks by combining a single team demonstration with minimal, targeted human feedback on synthesized counterfactuals. A generative model produces plausible alternative team behaviors from the demonstration, highlighting coordination-critical decision points. A human is queried to indicate preferences or correct suboptimal actions in these counterfactual scenarios. This feedback loop iteratively refines the reward function to capture the underlying coordination principles, resulting in a policy that generalizes beyond the initial demonstration and is robust to state perturbations and novel team configurations.

ideas.title: Architecture-Aware Tokenizer Alignment for Robust FAST Integration in Diverse VLAs
ideas.description: This research proposes a method to safely and effectively integrate the FAST tokenizer with diverse Vision-Language-Action (VLA) architectures beyond pi0. The core innovation is a trainable Adapter for Tokenizer Alignment (ATA) that mitigates distribution shift by learning to project FAST's token outputs into a latent space suitable for the target model's architectural priors. The system is trained with a multi-objective loss that balances faithful visual reconstruction, alignment with the original training distribution, and downstream task performance. This approach enables the benefits of FAST's efficiency across transformer variants, diffusion models, and other VLAs while maintaining robustness and avoiding instability.

ideas.title: Orchestrating Multi-Agent Coordination via Emergent Role Assignment from a Single Demonstration
ideas.description: This research expands one-shot reward generation for multi-agent coordination by drawing an analogy from collective animal behavior. In systems like ant colonies, a solution demonstrated by a subset of the group is scaled through stigmergy—indirect coordination via modifying the environment (e.g., pheromone trails). This allows the collective to self-organize into functional roles without explicit instruction. We adapt this by using a single team demonstration to generate a shared spatial-temporal reward gradient (a 'digital pheromone' field). Each robot learns a policy conditioned on this shared field and local agent states, enabling decentralized coordination. Through reinforcement learning, agents will exhibit emergent role assignment and scalable collaboration for tasks beyond the initial demonstration, mimicking the robustness and flexibility of biological collectives.

ideas.title: Causal Adaptive Fusion of Multi-Modal Demonstrations for Domain-Invariant Reward Learning
ideas.description: This research develops a framework that adaptively fuses textual and video demonstrations based on their causal informativeness for the underlying task goal. It employs causal discovery methods to disentangle spurious perceptual correlations from invariant, reward-relevant features that are consistent across both demonstration domains and environmental shifts. The core innovation is a causal model that guides the adaptive fusion process, ensuring the synthesized reward function prioritizes invariant causal relationships over domain-specific visual or linguistic cues. This results in robust reward functions that generalize to novel domains where traditional multi-modal fusion methods would fail due to deceptive correlations.

ideas.title: Causal Invariant Reward Learning via Domain Adaptation from Ecology
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from ecology, where scientists identify a species' fundamental niche—the invariant causal drivers of its presence across environments—by applying causal discovery methods like Invariant Causal Prediction (ICP) to observational data from multiple regions. Analogously, we propose using ICP on multi-domain demonstration datasets (e.g., from simulation, different physical robots, varied human demonstrators) to discover a minimal set of state-action features that have a stable, invariant causal relationship with the expert's demonstrated optimal actions. This process filters out spurious, domain-dependent correlations, yielding a robust, causally-grounded reward function that captures the true intent of the demonstrations and transfers reliably to new, unseen domains.

ideas.title: Causal Multi-Agent Coordination: Discovering Invariant Team Reward Structures from Single Demonstrations
ideas.description: This research fuses causal discovery with multi-agent learning from demonstration to solve collaborative tasks. It uses causal inference methods on a single team demonstration to identify the invariant structural relationships and causal dependencies between agents' actions that are necessary for task success, distinguishing them from incidental correlations. This discovered causal coordination graph then serves as a robust, transferable reward function generator, enabling the training of a new, potentially heterogeneous, multi-robot team in a different domain without additional demonstrations. The core innovation is achieving scalable and generalizable multi-agent coordination from minimal data by grounding the reward in causal team dynamics rather than observed agent trajectories.

ideas.title: Adversarial Tokenizer Pre-training via Domain Randomization
ideas.description: This idea adapts the principle of Domain Randomization from robotics simulation-to-real transfer to the problem of tokenizer training. Instead of training on ideal, curated text, the tokenizer is trained on a corpus where key linguistic features—such as syntactic structure, word frequency distributions, noise patterns, and stylistic markers—are procedurally generated and randomized. This adversarial pre-training aims to prevent the tokenizer from overfitting to any specific data characteristic, pushing it to discover more robust, fundamental units of segmentation. The core research question is whether this 'hardened' tokenizer demonstrates superior adaptability and performance when subsequently transferred and fine-tuned on real, high-quality language data trajectories, compared to tokenizers trained on standard or optimized corpora.

ideas.title: Task-Optimal Adaptive Tokenization via Differentiable Information Bottleneck
ideas.description: This research develops a framework for learning adaptive tokenization policies that are directly optimized for end-task performance under an information-theoretic constraint. Instead of relying on handcrafted heuristics for complexity, the system employs a differentiable relaxation of token merging and splitting operations, allowing a small controller network to dynamically adjust token granularity. The controller is trained jointly with the model using a rate-distortion objective, where the 'rate' is the effective bitrate of the adaptive token sequence and the 'distortion' is the task loss. This results in a tokenization strategy that provably preserves task-relevant information while aggressively compressing redundant or irrelevant details, leading to more robust and computationally efficient representations.

ideas.title: Causally-Factored Tokenization for Action-Observation Sequences
ideas.description: This approach introduces a structured tokenization that explicitly separates actions and observations into parallel streams to preserve their distinct causal roles. A causal attention mechanism is applied across streams, allowing the model to learn the temporal dependencies where past actions influence future observations, but not vice-versa. This factorization prevents the conflation of causes and effects while still enabling a unified model of the interaction loop. The result is a representation that inherently supports more robust planning, counterfactual reasoning, and credit assignment in sequential decision-making tasks.

ideas.title: Physics-Constrained Symbolic Regression for Feasible Action Primitives
ideas.description: This approach embeds a differentiable physics simulator or dynamics model as a hard constraint within the symbolic regression process. Candidate expressions for action trajectories are not just evaluated on reconstruction error but are also passed through a feasibility checker that ensures they respect actuator limits, stability criteria, and the agent's equations of motion. The regression objective is modified to jointly optimize for compactness, accuracy, and a feasibility loss, ensuring the discovered primitives are directly executable. This results in a library of action primitives that are both mathematically compact and guaranteed to be dynamically plausible for the target robotic or physical system.

ideas.title: Continuous Video Reward Learning via Streaming Signal Processing
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from Digital Signal Processing (DSP) for streaming data to the problem of scaling reward generation to continuous video streams. The analogous DSP solution uses sliding window transforms, adaptive filtering, and online compression to process infinite signals in real-time by maintaining a dynamic model of essential features. Adapted to lifelong learning from human demonstrations, the method treats the video stream as a temporal signal. A neural architecture with sliding attention windows and an adaptive compression module will continuously extract and update a compact 'reward basis' model. This model prioritizes novel or information-dense video segments for learning, discarding redundant frames, thereby enabling efficient, scalable, and never-ending reward function learning from an unbounded flow of demonstration videos without catastrophic forgetting or unbounded memory growth.

ideas.title: Autonomous Detection and Adaptation to Latent Goal Shifts via Meta-Learning and Probabilistic World-Models
ideas.description: This system autonomously handles environments where task objectives change without notification. It employs a meta-learner to monitor for anomalies in the observation and learning dynamics, signaling potential goal shifts. A probabilistic world-model then generates candidate new goal interpretations, which are tested through constrained, safe exploration. Finally, the agent uses this evidence to reconfigure its policy and reward estimator, enabling robust adaptation to latent, non-stationary objectives.

ideas.title: Temporally-Coherent and Causality-Constrained VQ for Sequential Action Representation
ideas.description: This approach adapts vector quantization for action representation by explicitly modeling the temporal structure of behavior. It trains a VQ-VAE where the encoder processes action sequences, and the discrete codes are regularized by a learned autoregressive prior that captures temporal dependencies. A novel causality-aware commitment loss is added, using a forward dynamics model to ensure the quantized action representation remains plausible under environmental constraints. This results in a discrete action space that is both compressible and suitable for planning or policy learning over long horizons.

ideas.title: Joint Action-Observation Tokens via Sensorimotor Contingency Theory
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from cognitive neuroscience and ecological psychology, specifically sensorimotor contingency theory, this idea challenges the assumption that actions and observations should be tokenized separately. The analogous solution treats perception and action as an inseparable unit, defined by the lawful sensory changes actions produce. Adapted to AI research, this leads to the development of a unified tokenizer that outputs 'sensorimotor tokens.' These tokens represent the contingent relationship between an action and the resulting perceptual change, forcing the model's fundamental representations to inherently encode the dynamics and affordances of the agent-environment interaction loop, rather than treating actions as isolated outputs.

ideas.title: Active Causal Reward Learning through Targeted Interventional Experimentation
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond passive causal discovery from observational demonstrations by embedding an active experimentation loop. The system proposes and executes targeted interventions—such as perturbing environmental factors or agent actions—within and across training domains to collect interventional data. This data is used to iteratively test, falsify, and update causal models of the reward, rigorously identifying features whose causal relationship to the reward remains invariant under intervention. The resulting reward function is grounded in verified causal mechanisms, ensuring robustness when deployed in novel domains where mere correlational invariances may fail.

ideas.title: Curriculum-Aligned Tokenizer Transfer via Iterative Data Selection
ideas.description: This research tests whether a tokenizer initially trained on low-quality or simulated data can be effectively adapted to a high-quality target domain through a guided curriculum. The system uses a small anchor set of real, high-quality data to train a lightweight model that scores and selects samples from the original suboptimal training corpus, creating a synthetic curriculum that bridges the distributional gap. The tokenizer is then progressively fine-tuned on this selected sequence, and its final performance is evaluated on held-out high-quality trajectories. This approach directly addresses the alignment problem by using the target domain to steer the tokenizer's exposure during training, rather than assuming a naive transfer.

ideas.title: Adaptive Tokenization via Perceptual Control Theory
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution of dynamic perceptual resolution from psychophysics and sensory neuroscience to the problem of adaptive tokenization bitrates. In the human visual system, perceptual load theory dictates that processing fidelity is adjusted based on task complexity, filtering details under high load and increasing resolution for precision tasks. The adapted idea is to develop a gating mechanism for language model tokenizers that mimics this behavior. This mechanism would dynamically adjust tokenization granularity (bitrate) in real-time: employing coarse, low-fidelity tokenization for high-speed or routine processing phases, and switching to fine-grained, high-fidelity tokenization for complex, novel, or precision-critical task segments. The goal is to create a tokenizer that optimally balances computational efficiency and representational accuracy based on the immediate processing demands of the model.

ideas.title: A Universal Interface for Tokenizer Integration in Vision-Language Architectures
ideas.description: This idea proposes solving the challenge of integrating the FAST tokenizer with diverse Vision-Language-Action (VLA) architectures (e.g., various transformers, diffusion variants) by drawing an analogy from bioinformatics. In genomics, the SAM/BAM file format acts as a universal interface between many sequence alignment algorithms and downstream analysis tools, decoupling their development. Adapting this solution, the research would design a standardized 'Visual Token Interface' (VTI)—an intermediate data representation and API specification. The FAST tokenizer would be extended to produce outputs compliant with this VTI, and VLA models beyond pi0 would be designed or wrapped with a lightweight adapter to consume the VTI format. This creates a modular ecosystem where tokenizers and VLAs can evolve independently while maintaining compatibility, significantly accelerating experimentation and deployment of new model-tokenizer pairs.

ideas.title: Adaptive Reward Shaping via Immune System-Inspired Memory and Meta-Plasticity
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from immunology's adaptive immune system to the RL problem of dynamic goals. The analogous problem is defending against an ever-changing set of pathogens, solved via immunological memory and meta-plasticity for novel threats. The adapted idea proposes an agent architecture with a 'reward memory' for fast recall of policies for recurring objectives, coupled with a meta-plastic component that re-shapes the agent's internal reward interpretation and policy search strategy when a completely new goal is detected. This enables efficient one-shot adaptation in non-stationary environments by balancing rapid recall with flexible generation of new behavioral strategies.

ideas.title: Apply Codebook-Based Compression from Speech Coding for Discrete Policy Learning
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from speech coding, specifically codebook-based methods like Code-Excited Linear Prediction (CELP). In CELP, a continuous speech signal is compressed by selecting entries from a discrete codebook of excitation vectors combined with linear predictive filters, balancing efficiency and reconstruction fidelity. Adapted to reinforcement learning, we propose incorporating a vector-quantized bottleneck within a policy network. The policy's continuous latent representation is quantized by selecting the nearest entry from a learned discrete codebook. This discrete code is then decoded into the final action, applying the compression and discretization principles of speech codecs to create more efficient, structured, and generalizable discrete action representations for policy learning.

ideas.title: Robust Reward Modeling via Signal Denoising and Outlier-Aware Filtering
ideas.description: This idea enhances reward model robustness to noisy demonstrations by drawing an analogy from signal processing in seismology. The analogous problem is isolating a true seismic signal from recordings contaminated by environmental noise and sensor faults, solved using wavelet denoising and outlier-aware statistical filtering. Adapted to RL, we treat a demonstration trajectory as a temporal signal. The research idea is to develop a reward model that applies spectral or learned decomposition to separate presumed expert intent from sub-optimal noise, and integrates a robust loss function (e.g., based on Huber loss or attention weights) to automatically down-weight outlier state-action pairs during training. This dual approach should allow the model to learn the underlying reward function even from single, imperfect demonstrations.
